## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  Test-infrastructure fixture; mechanical once shape is set. A cheap cloud-build
  probe — confirms the build/retrieve path yields a runnable artifact, with no
  crucible apparatus.
  
  ## Goal
  Prove the cloud-depot build-and-retrieve path yields a *runnable* artifact:
  ordain (conjure-mode) `rbev-busybox` → summon → bare container-runtime run of a
  degenerate command (assert exit 0) → abjure. Standing depot, no per-run levy.
  
  ## Suite placement — sibling to skirmish, NOT a member of it
  This is a new standing-depot scenario-chain suite named `dogfight`, sibling to
  `skirmish` in the operator-precondition family (both reuse a standing
  operator-levied depot via the `canonical-invest` idiom — no levy, no unmake).
  The two differ on the crucible axis and that difference is the whole point:
  `skirmish` charges the four crucibles to prove containment; `dogfight` charges
  NO crucible and proves only the cloud-build→summon→run path. Do NOT fold this
  fixture into the `skirmish` array — that would surround the crucible-free probe
  with the exact crucible work it exists to skip. It is emphatically NOT a new
  dependency tier "between service and crucible" — the dependency-tier axis
  (fast/service/crucible/complete) is a lattice whose top, `complete`, already
  means cloud+runtime; this lives on the orthogonal standing-depot scenario axis
  where `gauntlet` and `skirmish` already sit.
  
  ## Strongest leverage — reuse, don't rebuild
  `rbtdrc_hallmark_lifecycle` (rbtdrc_crucible.rs) already does ordain-rbev-busybox
  → … → abjure, including the BURE_CONFIRM-skip abjure. This fixture IS that, with
  the registry-inventory middle (audit/rekon) swapped for summon + run. Take the
  no-levy standing-depot idiom and the precondition probes from
  `RBTDRK_CASES_CANONICAL_INVEST` (rbtdrk_canonical.rs). For the bare run, model on
  `rbtdrc_image_layers` — the existing precedent for shelling a raw
  container-runtime command from a fixture.
  
  ## Locked constraints
  - **No crucible.** Proves build/depot/access viability, NOT containment (the
    crucible's orthogonal axis). A bare `<runtime> run --rm <ref> true` suffices —
    no sentry/pentacle/bottle/network. busybox's default cmd is `sh`, so pass an
    explicit `true` to get a clean exit-0 executability proof.
  - **Runtime hardcoded to docker behind ONE named constant** (podman deferred).
    The Director-governed runtime-regime-field decision rides with podman, after
    ₣BS settles the regime/credential shape — do NOT add a regime field now.
  - Presumes an operator-levied standing depot with director + retriever already
    invested; carry precondition probes like canonical-invest does.
  
  ## One unknown to resolve at mount (deliberately not pre-baked)
  How `summon` (rbw-fs RetrieverSummonsHallmark) names the locally-pulled image —
  the run needs that ref. Read it from the summon impl or its emitted burv fact.
  
  ## Suite wiring
  Mint a `dogfight` suite paralleling `skirmish` in rbte_engine.sh:
  `ZRBTE_SUITE_DOGFIGHT` array, the `dogfight)` resolver case arm, and the
  `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.dogfight.sh` tabtarget. A single-fixture suite is fine —
  the name is the operator's memorable batch handle. (A `rbw-t?` Qualify wrapper
  paralleling QualifySkirmish is optional and deferred — not part of done.)
  
  ## Registration
  New RBTDRM_FIXTURE_* const + fixtures roster entry + required-colophons map
  (must include summon alongside ordain/abjure). StateProgressing disposition —
  cases are sequentially dependent (conjure→summon→run→abjure).
  
  ## Done
  A fixture + single-case run path that ordains busybox, summons it, runs a
  degenerate command proving executability, and abjures — green against a standing
  depot, no crucible charged, reachable via the new `dogfight` suite tabtarget.

719663612  ₢BMAAJ    Retire the tabtarget dispatch token; name the launcher in the BURD_LAUNCHER config line instead. Every tabtarget now carries a byte-identical, token-free exec into z-launcher.sh, with launcher identity (and any NO_LOG/INTERACTIVE flags) confined to a BURD_* config block between shebang and exec. Generator (zbuut_write_tabtarget) emits 'export BURD_LAUNCHER=<basename>' as line 2 and drops the rbml_/buml_ owner-prefix case; trampoline (z-launcher.sh) reads the basename from BURD_LAUNCHER (fail-loud if unset), resolves it directly under rbml_launchers/, forwards all args (was ${@:2}), and drops the *ml_ strip + path re-export; qualifier (buq_tabtargets) requires the new shape (line2 BURD_LAUNCHER resolving to a launcher file, constant exec, min 3 lines) and drops sprue/*ml_ validation; burd_regime decl reworded path->basename. Converted all 215 non-exempt tabtargets via one idempotent awk pass (exec bit preserved; z-launcher.sh + buw-SI.StationInit.sh exempt). Docs: full re-derivation of BCG 'Tabtarget Path Indirection' to the BURD_LAUNCHER contract, pruned buml_ (zero referents) from the BUK include, narrowed rbml_ to its directory meaning in CLAUDE.md + rbk-acronyms. Done gate: QualifyFast green (215 checked / 2 exempt); live dispatch smoke across plain (rbw), NO_LOG (buw), INTERACTIVE (rbw) paths. Operator approved raising the record size_limit to admit the legitimate 215-file conversion bulk.                                                                                                                                                                           
1a3da826c  ₢BMAAI    Theurge commit-hygiene guard: stop hallmark/yoke commits from sweeping unrelated working-tree edits. (1) Extract the porcelain clean-tree check into shared rbtdre_engine::rbtdre_tree_clean; pristine Class-A now delegates to it (messages preserved). (2) Suite run-start guard in main.rs rbtdb_run_suite fatals on a dirty tree before any fixture work; single-case mode (crucible-debug loop) intentionally left unguarded. (3) Re-signature rbtdro_git_commit to take an explicit files list and git add -- <files> instead of git add -A; porcelain pre-check scoped to owned files so idempotent no-op steps still skip cleanly. (4) All 10 call sites thread their change sets (consumer rbrn.env for hallmark drives, vessel rbrv.env for enshrine/locator writes, enumerated all-vessels set for the wildcard yoke) via new path helpers rbtdro_nameplate_rbrn_path / rbtdro_vessel_rbrv_path / rbtdro_all_vessel_rbrv_paths; sets verified against historical commits. graft-demo (terminal, no repo write) given ownership of its vessel rbrv.env, normally a clean no-op. Verification (rbtd build/test + dispatch smoke) pending operator clearance to run scripts.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               
64c141375  ₢BMAAG    Prune two now-orphaned IAM helpers from rbgu_Utility.sh, dead after the rbgi_add_project_iam_role read-back verify-loop removal (that removal landed in 24dc4c6a3, swept into a concurrent skirmish ordain commit): rbgu_role_member_exists_predicate (the getIamPolicy role+member existence predicate the verify loop polled) and rbgu_http_json_ok (whose only caller was that verify loop). Zero remaining call sites across Tools/ and tt/. Load-bearing-discipline dead-code cleanup; bash -n clean. Context: the verify loop was removed because, against a standing depot, repeated divest/invest leaves same-email deleted:serviceAccount tombstones (accepted -- re-levy is quota-limited), and the email->uid read reconciliation can lag getIamPolicy past the 90s deadline, producing a false 'verify timeout' on a grant whose setIamPolicy already returned 200. RBSCIP already abandoned read-back-as-propagation-gate (2026-03-03: read-back proves policy intent, not enforcement); SA-establishment waits (rbgu_poll_until_ok SA-by-email, phase-1 400 member-visibility tolerance) are retained.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               
70dc76b8d  ₢BMAAG    Fix cross-arch tripwire portability (spook surfaced while verifying BMAAG's live skirmish gate). rbrd_inscribe built the FROM-scratch tripwire with a bare `docker build` (no --platform), baking the levying host's native arch; a Mac(arm64)-levied depot's tripwire was then unpullable on amd64 hosts and Cloud Build, which is exactly what broke skirmish's onboarding-sequence inscribe-reliquary ('no matching manifest for linux/amd64'). Pinned --platform "${RBGC_BUILD_RUNNER_PLATFORM}" on inscribe build + check pull/create (rbndb_base.sh) -- symmetric single-platform, since the image is FROM-scratch data (rbrd.env only, never executed) so its arch is semantically irrelevant but docker's manifest selection on pull still demands a match. Rejected the arch-tolerant-check variant (parse the inscribed platform and pull it) as permanent state-space pollution -- a fallback for a wrong-arch state the inscribe-side pin makes unreachable. Also collapsed the rbrd.env magic string per BCG tinder discipline: new RBCC_rbrd_basename="rbrd.env" tinder constant in rbcc_Constants.sh, RBCC_rbrd_file recomposed tinder-on-tinder from it, and every rbndb basename/in-image (/rbrd.env) literal routed through ${RBCC_rbrd_basename} -- the string now appears once in bash. The live bhm depot's existing arm64 tripwire was manually re-pushed as amd64 from verified-byte-identical content (deliberate immutability-guard bypass; drift baseline preserved; no re-levy available); native amd64 pull + create + cp + cmp confirmed working. Parse-clean (bash -n); shellcheck deferred (unavailable in sandbox).

Docket:
  ## Character
  Test-infrastructure fixture; mechanical once shape is set. A cheap cloud-build
  probe — confirms the build/retrieve path yields a runnable artifact, with no
  crucible apparatus.
  
  ## Goal
  Prove the cloud-depot build-and-retrieve path yields a *runnable* artifact:
  ordain (conjure-mode) `rbev-busybox` → summon → bare container-runtime run of a
  degenerate command (assert exit 0) → abjure. Standing depot, no per-run levy.
  
  ## Suite placement — sibling to skirmish, NOT a member of it
  This is a new standing-depot scenario-chain suite named `dogfight`, sibling to
  `skirmish` in the operator-precondition family (both reuse a standing
  operator-levied depot via the `canonical-invest` idiom — no levy, no unmake).
  The two differ on the crucible axis and that difference is the whole point:
  `skirmish` charges the four crucibles to prove containment; `dogfight` charges
  NO crucible and proves only the cloud-build→summon→run path. Do NOT fold this
  fixture into the `skirmish` array — that would surround the crucible-free probe
  with the exact crucible work it exists to skip. It is emphatically NOT a new
  dependency tier "between service and crucible" — the dependency-tier axis
  (fast/service/crucible/complete) is a lattice whose top, `complete`, already
  means cloud+runtime; this lives on the orthogonal standing-depot scenario axis
  where `gauntlet` and `skirmish` already sit.
  
  ## Strongest leverage — reuse, don't rebuild
  `rbtdrc_hallmark_lifecycle` (rbtdrc_crucible.rs) already does ordain-rbev-busybox
  → … → abjure, including the BURE_CONFIRM-skip abjure. This fixture IS that, with
  the registry-inventory middle (audit/rekon) swapped for summon + run. Take the
  no-levy standing-depot idiom and the precondition probes from
  `RBTDRK_CASES_CANONICAL_INVEST` (rbtdrk_canonical.rs). For the bare run, model on
  `rbtdrc_image_layers` — the existing precedent for shelling a raw
  container-runtime command from a fixture.
  
  ## Locked constraints
  - **No crucible.** Proves build/depot/access viability, NOT containment (the
    crucible's orthogonal axis). A bare `<runtime> run --rm <ref> true` suffices —
    no sentry/pentacle/bottle/network. busybox's default cmd is `sh`, so pass an
    explicit `true` to get a clean exit-0 executability proof.
  - **Runtime hardcoded to docker behind ONE named constant** (podman deferred).
    The Director-governed runtime-regime-field decision rides with podman, after
    ₣BS settles the regime/credential shape — do NOT add a regime field now.
  - Presumes an operator-levied standing depot with director + retriever already
    invested; carry precondition probes like canonical-invest does.
  
  ## One unknown to resolve at mount (deliberately not pre-baked)
  How `summon` (rbw-fs RetrieverSummonsHallmark) names the locally-pulled image —
  the run needs that ref. Read it from the summon impl or its emitted burv fact.
  
  ## Suite wiring
  Mint a `dogfight` suite paralleling `skirmish` in rbte_engine.sh:
  `ZRBTE_SUITE_DOGFIGHT` array, the `dogfight)` resolver case arm, and the
  `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.dogfight.sh` tabtarget. A single-fixture suite is fine —
  the name is the operator's memorable batch handle. (A `rbw-t?` Qualify wrapper
  paralleling QualifySkirmish is optional and deferred — not part of done.)
  
  ## Registration
  New RBTDRM_FIXTURE_* const + fixtures roster entry + required-colophons map
  (must include summon alongside ordain/abjure). StateProgressing disposition —
  cases are sequentially dependent (conjure→summon→run→abjure).
  
  ## Done
  A fixture + single-case run path that ordains busybox, summons it, runs a
  degenerate command proving executability, and abjures — green against a standing
  depot, no crucible charged, reachable via the new `dogfight` suite tabtarget.

1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BM
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Regime enums in this project carry their values as bare English words —
6	`conjure`, `bind`, `graft` for `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE`; `tether`, `airgap` for
7	`RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`; node-regime modes (`disabled`/`enabled`/`global`/
8	`allowlist`) for the `RBRN_*` family. These bare values appear scattered
9	across bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build steps), and Rust (test
10	fixtures), with no cluster-discoverable marker tying any one value to its
11	enum family. This makes mode renames cross-language hand-search-and-replace
12	work, prone to silent drift.
13	
14	There is one prior in-tree exemplar of the prefixed-value approach:
15	`bubep_linux | bubep_mac | bubep_windows` for `BURN_PLATFORM`. It works but
16	uses a different shape from what this heat adopts; harmonization is part of
17	the sweep.
18	
19	The inventory pace has been completed (see heat log / slate commit
20	messages for depth). This paddock records the resolved scope and the
21	durable findings; line-numbered site lists deliberately live in the
22	discovery recipe, not here.
23	
24	## MCM grounding — "sprue" is `mcm_sprue`, prefix discipline makes it an inlay
25	
26	"Sprue" here is not a coined word — it is the MCM quoin `mcm_sprue`: *a
27	literal wire-level token representing a serialized name — the exact
28	character sequence as it appears in a wire format (a JSON property key, an
29	API field name, a protocol identifier).* Our regime enum values are
30	textbook sprues: `rbnve_conjure` is the verbatim token that appears in
31	`rbrv.env`, in the `_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE` Cloud Build substitution, in
32	build-info JSON, and in Python/shell comparisons.
33	
34	MCM also states: *a sprue that follows the prefix naming discipline is also
35	an `mcm_inlay`* (prefix-recognized, not lexicon-catalogued). So precisely,
36	the values are *already* sprues even while bare; what this heat adds is the
37	prefix discipline that makes each sprue **also an inlay**. The spec pace
38	(₢BMAAA) should cite `mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay` for grounding rather than
39	inventing rationale.
40	
41	Note on a sibling usage: ₣BK independently uses "sprue" for tabtarget
42	dispatch tokens (`{owner}ml_{launcher-id}`, e.g. `rbml_rbw`) and wrote a
43	local gloss in BCG/CLAUDE.md. That is *also* a valid `mcm_sprue` instance
44	(a literal dispatch-wire token) — not a competing definition, a sibling
45	one. Both heats correctly name wire-level literal tokens "sprue." ₣BK's BCG
46	text would ideally cite `mcm_sprue` rather than read as a freshly-minted
47	concept, but that is ₣BK's documentation concern, not a blocker here.
48	
49	## Convention — locked
50	
51	Pattern: `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>` where:
52	
53	- `<proj>` — project prefix (`rb` for Recipe Bottle, `bu` for BUK)
54	- `<n>`    — single letter signaling "this is a regime namespace value"
55	             (chosen `n` for "namespace", contrasting `r` which is taken
56	             for the regime-variable family `rbr*`/`bur*`)
57	- `<regime>` — single letter mirroring the regime family
58	             (`v` = vessel, `n` = node, `c` = config, `s` = station,
59	             `e` = environment, etc.)
60	- `<e>`    — single letter signaling "this is an enum value"
61	- `_<value>` — the canonical value name
62	
63	Full target table (all in-scope enums):
64	
65	| Variable                 | Regime | Bare → Sprue                                          |
66	|--------------------------|:------:|-------------------------------------------------------|
67	| RBRV_VESSEL_MODE         | v      | bind/conjure/graft → `rbnve_bind`/`_conjure`/`_graft` |
68	| RBRV_EGRESS_MODE         | v      | tether/airgap → `rbnve_tether`/`_airgap`              |
69	| RBRN_ENTRY_MODE          | n      | disabled/enabled → `rbnne_disabled`/`_enabled`        |
70	| RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE     | n      | disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_*`                 |
71	| RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE  | n      | disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_*`                 |
72	| BURN_PLATFORM            | n      | bubep_linux/mac/windows → `bunne_linux`/`_mac`/`_windows` |
73	
74	Family-sharing is a deliberate property, not an accident: vessel-mode and
75	egress-mode share `rbnve_` (both vessel-regime); the three node-mode enums
76	share `rbnne_`, so `rbnne_disabled` is intentionally voiced by ENTRY,
77	DNS, and ACCESS alike. Uniqueness lives in the full quoin + the owning
78	variable, not in the 5-char prefix. A single grep per family returns the
79	whole cluster.
80	
81	## Scope decisions — resolved
82	
83	- **RBRV_VESSEL_MODE + RBRV_EGRESS_MODE** → `rbnve_*`. In.
84	- **RBRN_ENTRY_MODE / UPLINK_DNS_MODE / UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE** → `rbnne_*`.
85	  In. Their values are pure comparison tokens with no second life, so the
86	  prefix is free (no indirection needed).
87	- **BURN_PLATFORM** → harmonize `bubep_*` to `bunne_*` in the same sweep.
88	  Decided harmonize over grandfather, per load-bearing-complexity
89	  discipline — eliminates the two-shape wart rather than freezing it.
90	- **RBRN_RUNTIME (`docker`/`podman`) — EXCLUDED, stays bare.** Its value
91	  is consumed as the literal executable name (`command -v "${RBRN_RUNTIME}"`,
92	  `${RBRN_RUNTIME} image inspect`). Adopting the convention would require a
93	  permanent value→binary indirection layer at every dispatch site — a
94	  runtime cost paid for grep-discoverability of two values that
95	  `RBRN_RUNTIME` itself already marks. The convention would cost more than
96	  it returns. This is the one regime enum that does not earn the prefix;
97	  the exclusion is deliberate, not an oversight.
98	- **BURE_VERBOSE / BURE_COLOR** — out (bare ints, different problem class;
99	  unchanged from original framing).
100	
101	## Comment / use-site classification — judgment work
102	
103	Bare-word grep on a value yields four classes. The migration paces walk
104	the candidate list and classify each hit:
105	
106	1. **Phase / concept references** — "the conjure phase", "the airgap
107	   build" — refer to the workflow, not the enum value. **Leave alone.**
108	2. **Enum-value references** — equality tests, case arms, JSON literals,
109	   `vessel mode is conjure`, defaults — the actual variable value.
110	   **Migrate.**
111	3. **Identifier embeds** — `RBSAC-ark_conjure.adoc`, `rbho_conjure_*`,
112	   vessel sigils `rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`, test-step labels like
113	   `ordain-conjure` — the bare word is part of an identifier. **Leave
114	   alone.**
115	4. **Coupled derived strings** — the value drives a derived token that
116	   lives in resource-name space and must stay bare even though it is
117	   semantically the enum value. **Leave the derived string bare; migrate
118	   only the dispatch that selects it.** Two confirmed witnesses:
119	   - egress → worker-pool suffixes (`RBGC_POOL_SUFFIX_*`, the `-tether`/
120	     `-airgap` fragments in pool paths)
121	   - vessel-mode → hallmark prefixes (`RBGC_HALLMARK_PREFIX_*` = `c`/`b`/`g`)
122	   Because of class 4, even a class-2 case arm is not blind find/replace:
123	   the arm becomes `rbnve_tether)` while the pool string it maps to stays
124	   `-tether`.
125	
126	### Three judgment traps a naive grep misses
127	
128	- **Default-value defaults** — `${z_mode:-conjure}` is class-2 but a
129	  `== "conjure"` grep skips it. The recipe must also grep `:-conjure`,
130	  `:-bind`, etc.
131	- **AsciiDoc wire-value tables read like prose** — a spec table cell
132	  documenting `(conjure, bind, or graft)` as a substitution value is
133	  class-2 (migrate), not class-1 prose. Easy to misclassify and leave
134	  stale.
135	- **Rust noise is almost all class-3** — the onboarding-scenario fixtures
136	  are full of step labels (`ordain-conjure`, `wrest-bind`) and sigil
137	  consts that stay bare. Only the `export RBRV_*_MODE="..."` baseline
138	  strings in the fast-fixture file actually migrate; that pace rebuilds
139	  the Rust crate (`tt/vow-b`).
140	
141	### Noise ranking (classification effort)
142	
143	Counterintuitively `conjure` is the noisiest value (heavy phase/operation
144	prose), then `bind` (English-word noise: bind-mount, IP bind), then
145	`graft`; `tether`/`airgap` are the cleanest (~10 sites each, mostly
146	class-2 or class-4). This is why egress is the cheap pilot and vessel-mode
147	is the heavy classification grind.
148	
149	## Cloud Build detection gap — vessel-mode acceptance gate
150	
151	Vessel-mode's value crosses a substitution boundary
152	(`_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE` / `_RBGV_VESSEL_MODE`) into a **remote execution
153	context** — Cloud Build step shells and Python steps compare against the
154	literal value. The local `regime-validation` net catches the
155	declaration↔Rust-fixture lockstep, but it does **not** exercise the
156	remote-side comparisons. A missed step-shell or Python site fails silently
157	until an actual conjure/graft cloud build runs.
158	
159	**Consequence:** for the vessel-mode pace, "gauntlet green" is not
160	sufficient proof of done. Its acceptance gate is an actual **conjure +
161	graft cloud build** (the full ordain path), not just the fast/crucible
162	suites.
163	
164	## Discovery recipe
165	
166	- `grep -rn 'buv_enum_enroll' Tools/buk/ Tools/rbk/` — authoritative
167	  declarations; cross-walk `buv_gate_enroll` invocations to confirm no
168	  enum missed.
169	- Per value V: `grep -rn '\bV\b' Tools/ tt/` filtered by extension for
170	  code sites; also `grep -rn ':-V\b'` for default-value defaults.
171	- Comment/prose: `grep -rn '#.*\bV\b'` (bash/Python), `'//.*\bV\b'`
172	  (Rust), and scan `Tools/*/vov_veiled/*.adoc` for prose + wire-value
173	  tables.
174	- Substitution boundary: trace `_RBG[AV]_VESSEL_MODE` /
175	  `_RBG[AV]_EGRESS*` through `rbfd_*`/`rbfv_*` step assembly into the
176	  `rbgja*`/`rbgjv*` Python+shell steps.
177	
178	## Posture
179	
180	**Burn-bridges migration.** No backwards compatibility. Depots reform on
181	execution, vessels re-charge, regime files get edited in place. Operator
182	pre-flight: `rbw-MZ` before sweep, all crucibles quenched.
183	
184	**Deep-test stragglers.** `regime-validation`, `dockerfile-hygiene`, and
185	`gauntlet` catch any local bare-value site, because the enum-validation
186	gate rejects a non-prefixed value once the declaration is updated. The
187	remote Cloud Build comparisons are the exception — see the detection gap
188	above.
189	
190	## Heat-shape note — ₣BK relationship (BK coding landed)
191	
192	₣BK (moorings-cutover) swept essentially this heat's file surface
193	(`Tools/rbk/**/*.sh`, `rbtd/src/*.rs`, `RBS*.adoc`) — but for path literals,
194	not enum values. The two heats are **semantically orthogonal** (different
195	concerns, mostly different lines). ₣BK's *coding* has landed (BKAAI
196	wrapped); the prior file-surface-churn concern is now historical — the tree
197	is settled and the re-validation confirmed the enum-value sites survived
198	intact.
199	
200	What remains and matters:
201	- **Start gate:** ₣BK's two-platform gauntlet acceptance is still pending.
202	  Begin this heat only after ₣BK's gauntlet passes on linux + macos and any
203	  cutover bugfixes land — so a known-good cutover baseline exists and every
204	  ₣BM gauntlet signal is unambiguously about enums, not the cutover.
205	- **Residual churn risk:** only late ₣BK bugfixes from its gauntlet runs.
206	  Low; re-grep before editing if BK commits land meanwhile.
207	- The bubep→bunne harmonize touches BUK files (₣BK's other subtree). ₣BK's
208	  BUK code has landed, so no hard coordination remains — just awareness of
209	  any late bugfix churn there.
210	
211	## What done looks like
212	
213	- All in-scope bare enum values replaced with sprue-prefixed values across
214	  bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build), and Rust; `RBRN_RUNTIME` left bare.
215	- `buv_enum_enroll` (and gated `buv_gate_enroll`) declarations updated to
216	  expect the new values; regime files (`rbrv.env`, `rbrn.env`, BURN
217	  profiles) updated in place.
218	- Class-4 derived strings (pool suffixes, hallmark prefixes) left bare;
219	  their selecting dispatch migrated.
220	- `regime-validation` + `dockerfile-hygiene` + `gauntlet` green.
221	- Vessel-mode additionally proven by a real conjure + graft cloud build.
222	- A single grep per family returns the complete cluster:
223	  `grep -rn rbnve_ Tools/`, `grep -rn rbnne_ Tools/`,
224	  `grep -rn bunne_ Tools/` — and `grep -rn bubep_` returns nothing.
225	
226	## Out of scope
227	
228	- Sprue convention applied to non-regime-enum domains (status codes, JSON
229	  wire-format keys, etc.) — separate heat.
230	- `RBRN_RUNTIME` (`docker`/`podman`) — executable-name coupling, excluded
231	  by decision above.
232	- `BURE_VERBOSE` / `BURE_COLOR` integer values — different problem class.
233	- Backwards-compatibility shims of any kind.
234	
235	## Pace shape (deferred to mount-time)
236	
237	Resolved cadence is per-variable-family, ordered cheap-pilot-first:
238	
239	1. Convention spec authoring — formalize the convention as a spec section,
240	   citing `mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay` for grounding, plus the class-4
241	   "coupled derived string stays bare" rule (two witnesses) and the
242	   `RBRN_RUNTIME` exclusion rationale.
243	2. Egress-mode pilot — `rbnve_tether`/`_airgap`. Smallest, cleanest;
244	   proves the mechanical pattern and the class-4 distinction.
245	3. Vessel-mode — `rbnve_bind`/`_conjure`/`_graft`. Heaviest; crosses
246	   bash/adoc/Python(Cloud Build)/Rust. Acceptance gate = real conjure +
247	   graft cloud build.
248	4. Node-mode — `rbnne_*` for ENTRY/DNS/ACCESS. High English-word noise,
249	   heavy classification.
250	5. bubep → bunne harmonize — BUK files; last slice.
251	6. Final regression sweep — gauntlet end-to-end + per-family grep audit.
252	
253	Slate as thin dockets at mount-time (depth in slate commit messages); do
254	not pre-cut detailed site lists from this paddock.
255	
256	# jjezs_pace ₢BMAAH
257	
258	## Character
259	Test-infrastructure fixture; mechanical once shape is set. A cheap cloud-build
260	probe — confirms the build/retrieve path yields a runnable artifact, with no
261	crucible apparatus.
262	
263	## Goal
264	Prove the cloud-depot build-and-retrieve path yields a *runnable* artifact:
265	ordain (conjure-mode) `rbev-busybox` → summon → bare container-runtime run of a
266	degenerate command (assert exit 0) → abjure. Standing depot, no per-run levy.
267	
268	## Suite placement — sibling to skirmish, NOT a member of it
269	This is a new standing-depot scenario-chain suite named `dogfight`, sibling to
270	`skirmish` in the operator-precondition family (both reuse a standing
271	operator-levied depot via the `canonical-invest` idiom — no levy, no unmake).
272	The two differ on the crucible axis and that difference is the whole point:
273	`skirmish` charges the four crucibles to prove containment; `dogfight` charges
274	NO crucible and proves only the cloud-build→summon→run path. Do NOT fold this
275	fixture into the `skirmish` array — that would surround the crucible-free probe
276	with the exact crucible work it exists to skip. It is emphatically NOT a new
277	dependency tier "between service and crucible" — the dependency-tier axis
278	(fast/service/crucible/complete) is a lattice whose top, `complete`, already
279	means cloud+runtime; this lives on the orthogonal standing-depot scenario axis
280	where `gauntlet` and `skirmish` already sit.
281	
282	## Strongest leverage — reuse, don't rebuild
283	`rbtdrc_hallmark_lifecycle` (rbtdrc_crucible.rs) already does ordain-rbev-busybox
284	→ … → abjure, including the BURE_CONFIRM-skip abjure. This fixture IS that, with
285	the registry-inventory middle (audit/rekon) swapped for summon + run. Take the
286	no-levy standing-depot idiom and the precondition probes from
287	`RBTDRK_CASES_CANONICAL_INVEST` (rbtdrk_canonical.rs). For the bare run, model on
288	`rbtdrc_image_layers` — the existing precedent for shelling a raw
289	container-runtime command from a fixture.
290	
291	## Locked constraints
292	- **No crucible.** Proves build/depot/access viability, NOT containment (the
293	  crucible's orthogonal axis). A bare `<runtime> run --rm <ref> true` suffices —
294	  no sentry/pentacle/bottle/network. busybox's default cmd is `sh`, so pass an
295	  explicit `true` to get a clean exit-0 executability proof.
296	- **Runtime hardcoded to docker behind ONE named constant** (podman deferred).
297	  The Director-governed runtime-regime-field decision rides with podman, after
298	  ₣BS settles the regime/credential shape — do NOT add a regime field now.
299	- Presumes an operator-levied standing depot with director + retriever already
300	  invested; carry precondition probes like canonical-invest does.
301	
302	## One unknown to resolve at mount (deliberately not pre-baked)
303	How `summon` (rbw-fs RetrieverSummonsHallmark) names the locally-pulled image —
304	the run needs that ref. Read it from the summon impl or its emitted burv fact.
305	
306	## Suite wiring
307	Mint a `dogfight` suite paralleling `skirmish` in rbte_engine.sh:
308	`ZRBTE_SUITE_DOGFIGHT` array, the `dogfight)` resolver case arm, and the
309	`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.dogfight.sh` tabtarget. A single-fixture suite is fine —
310	the name is the operator's memorable batch handle. (A `rbw-t?` Qualify wrapper
311	paralleling QualifySkirmish is optional and deferred — not part of done.)
312	
313	## Registration
314	New RBTDRM_FIXTURE_* const + fixtures roster entry + required-colophons map
315	(must include summon alongside ordain/abjure). StateProgressing disposition —
316	cases are sequentially dependent (conjure→summon→run→abjure).
317	
318	## Done
319	A fixture + single-case run path that ordains busybox, summons it, runs a
320	degenerate command proving executability, and abjures — green against a standing
321	depot, no crucible charged, reachable via the new `dogfight` suite tabtarget.
322

# jjezs_paddock ₣BM

## Context

Regime enums in this project carry their values as bare English words —
`conjure`, `bind`, `graft` for `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE`; `tether`, `airgap` for
`RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`; node-regime modes (`disabled`/`enabled`/`global`/
`allowlist`) for the `RBRN_*` family. These bare values appear scattered
across bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build steps), and Rust (test
fixtures), with no cluster-discoverable marker tying any one value to its
enum family. This makes mode renames cross-language hand-search-and-replace
work, prone to silent drift.

There is one prior in-tree exemplar of the prefixed-value approach:
`bubep_linux | bubep_mac | bubep_windows` for `BURN_PLATFORM`. It works but
uses a different shape from what this heat adopts; harmonization is part of
the sweep.

The inventory pace has been completed (see heat log / slate commit
messages for depth). This paddock records the resolved scope and the
durable findings; line-numbered site lists deliberately live in the
discovery recipe, not here.

## MCM grounding — "sprue" is `mcm_sprue`, prefix discipline makes it an inlay

"Sprue" here is not a coined word — it is the MCM quoin `mcm_sprue`: *a
literal wire-level token representing a serialized name — the exact
character sequence as it appears in a wire format (a JSON property key, an
API field name, a protocol identifier).* Our regime enum values are
textbook sprues: `rbnve_conjure` is the verbatim token that appears in
`rbrv.env`, in the `_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE` Cloud Build substitution, in
build-info JSON, and in Python/shell comparisons.

MCM also states: *a sprue that follows the prefix naming discipline is also
an `mcm_inlay`* (prefix-recognized, not lexicon-catalogued). So precisely,
the values are *already* sprues even while bare; what this heat adds is the
prefix discipline that makes each sprue **also an inlay**. The spec pace
(₢BMAAA) should cite `mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay` for grounding rather than
inventing rationale.

Note on a sibling usage: ₣BK independently uses "sprue" for tabtarget
dispatch tokens (`{owner}ml_{launcher-id}`, e.g. `rbml_rbw`) and wrote a
local gloss in BCG/CLAUDE.md. That is *also* a valid `mcm_sprue` instance
(a literal dispatch-wire token) — not a competing definition, a sibling
one. Both heats correctly name wire-level literal tokens "sprue." ₣BK's BCG
text would ideally cite `mcm_sprue` rather than read as a freshly-minted
concept, but that is ₣BK's documentation concern, not a blocker here.

## Convention — locked

Pattern: `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>` where:

- `<proj>` — project prefix (`rb` for Recipe Bottle, `bu` for BUK)
- `<n>`    — single letter signaling "this is a regime namespace value"
             (chosen `n` for "namespace", contrasting `r` which is taken
             for the regime-variable family `rbr*`/`bur*`)
- `<regime>` — single letter mirroring the regime family
             (`v` = vessel, `n` = node, `c` = config, `s` = station,
             `e` = environment, etc.)
- `<e>`    — single letter signaling "this is an enum value"
- `_<value>` — the canonical value name

Full target table (all in-scope enums):

| Variable                 | Regime | Bare → Sprue                                          |
|--------------------------|:------:|-------------------------------------------------------|
| RBRV_VESSEL_MODE         | v      | bind/conjure/graft → `rbnve_bind`/`_conjure`/`_graft` |
| RBRV_EGRESS_MODE         | v      | tether/airgap → `rbnve_tether`/`_airgap`              |
| RBRN_ENTRY_MODE          | n      | disabled/enabled → `rbnne_disabled`/`_enabled`        |
| RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE     | n      | disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_*`                 |
| RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE  | n      | disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_*`                 |
| BURN_PLATFORM            | n      | bubep_linux/mac/windows → `bunne_linux`/`_mac`/`_windows` |

Family-sharing is a deliberate property, not an accident: vessel-mode and
egress-mode share `rbnve_` (both vessel-regime); the three node-mode enums

## Scope decisions — resolved

- **RBRN_ENTRY_MODE / UPLINK_DNS_MODE / UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE** → `rbnne_*`.
  In. Their values are pure comparison tokens with no second life, so the
  prefix is free (no indirection needed).
- **BURN_PLATFORM** → harmonize `bubep_*` to `bunne_*` in the same sweep.
  Decided harmonize over grandfather, per load-bearing-complexity
  discipline — eliminates the two-shape wart rather than freezing it.
- **RBRN_RUNTIME (`docker`/`podman`) — EXCLUDED, stays bare.** Its value
  is consumed as the literal executable name (`command -v "${RBRN_RUNTIME}"`,

  permanent value→binary indirection layer at every dispatch site — a
  runtime cost paid for grep-discoverability of two values that
  `RBRN_RUNTIME` itself already marks. The convention would cost more than
  it returns. This is the one regime enum that does not earn the prefix;
  the exclusion is deliberate, not an oversight.
- **BURE_VERBOSE / BURE_COLOR** — out (bare ints, different problem class;
  unchanged from original framing).

## Comment / use-site classification — judgment work

Bare-word grep on a value yields four classes. The migration paces walk
the candidate list and classify each hit:

1. **Phase / concept references** — "the conjure phase", "the airgap
   build" — refer to the workflow, not the enum value. **Leave alone.**
2. **Enum-value references** — equality tests, case arms, JSON literals,
   `vessel mode is conjure`, defaults — the actual variable value.
   **Migrate.**
3. **Identifier embeds** — `RBSAC-ark_conjure.adoc`, `rbho_conjure_*`,
   vessel sigils `rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`, test-step labels like
   `ordain-conjure` — the bare word is part of an identifier. **Leave
   alone.**
4. **Coupled derived strings** — the value drives a derived token that
   lives in resource-name space and must stay bare even though it is
   semantically the enum value. **Leave the derived string bare; migrate
   only the dispatch that selects it.** Two confirmed witnesses:
   - egress → worker-pool suffixes (`RBGC_POOL_SUFFIX_*`, the `-tether`/
     `-airgap` fragments in pool paths)
   - vessel-mode → hallmark prefixes (`RBGC_HALLMARK_PREFIX_*` = `c`/`b`/`g`)
   Because of class 4, even a class-2 case arm is not blind find/replace:
   the arm becomes `rbnve_tether)` while the pool string it maps to stays
   `-tether`.

### Three judgment traps a naive grep misses

- **Default-value defaults** — `${z_mode:-conjure}` is class-2 but a
  `== "conjure"` grep skips it. The recipe must also grep `:-conjure`,
  `:-bind`, etc.
- **AsciiDoc wire-value tables read like prose** — a spec table cell
  documenting `(conjure, bind, or graft)` as a substitution value is
  class-2 (migrate), not class-1 prose. Easy to misclassify and leave
  stale.
- **Rust noise is almost all class-3** — the onboarding-scenario fixtures
  are full of step labels (`ordain-conjure`, `wrest-bind`) and sigil
  consts that stay bare. Only the `export RBRV_*_MODE="..."` baseline
  strings in the fast-fixture file actually migrate; that pace rebuilds
  the Rust crate (`tt/vow-b`).

### Noise ranking (classification effort)

Counterintuitively `conjure` is the noisiest value (heavy phase/operation
prose), then `bind` (English-word noise: bind-mount, IP bind), then
`graft`; `tether`/`airgap` are the cleanest (~10 sites each, mostly
class-2 or class-4). This is why egress is the cheap pilot and vessel-mode
is the heavy classification grind.

## Cloud Build detection gap — vessel-mode acceptance gate

Vessel-mode's value crosses a substitution boundary
(`_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE` / `_RBGV_VESSEL_MODE`) into a **remote execution
context** — Cloud Build step shells and Python steps compare against the
literal value. The local `regime-validation` net catches the
declaration↔Rust-fixture lockstep, but it does **not** exercise the
remote-side comparisons. A missed step-shell or Python site fails silently
until an actual conjure/graft cloud build runs.

**Consequence:** for the vessel-mode pace, "gauntlet green" is not
sufficient proof of done. Its acceptance gate is an actual **conjure +
graft cloud build** (the full ordain path), not just the fast/crucible
suites.

## Discovery recipe

- `grep -rn 'buv_enum_enroll' Tools/buk/ Tools/rbk/` — authoritative
  declarations; cross-walk `buv_gate_enroll` invocations to confirm no
  enum missed.
- Per value V: `grep -rn '\bV\b' Tools/ tt/` filtered by extension for
  code sites; also `grep -rn ':-V\b'` for default-value defaults.
- Comment/prose: `grep -rn '#.*\bV\b'` (bash/Python), `'//.*\bV\b'`
  (Rust), and scan `Tools/*/vov_veiled/*.adoc` for prose + wire-value
  tables.
- Substitution boundary: trace `_RBG[AV]_VESSEL_MODE` /
  `_RBG[AV]_EGRESS*` through `rbfd_*`/`rbfv_*` step assembly into the
  `rbgja*`/`rbgjv*` Python+shell steps.

## Posture

**Burn-bridges migration.** No backwards compatibility. Depots reform on
execution, vessels re-charge, regime files get edited in place. Operator
pre-flight: `rbw-MZ` before sweep, all crucibles quenched.

**Deep-test stragglers.** `regime-validation`, `dockerfile-hygiene`, and
`gauntlet` catch any local bare-value site, because the enum-validation
gate rejects a non-prefixed value once the declaration is updated. The
remote Cloud Build comparisons are the exception — see the detection gap
above.

## Heat-shape note — ₣BK relationship (BK coding landed)

₣BK (moorings-cutover) swept essentially this heat's file surface
(`Tools/rbk/**/*.sh`, `rbtd/src/*.rs`, `RBS*.adoc`) — but for path literals,
not enum values. The two heats are **semantically orthogonal** (different
concerns, mostly different lines). ₣BK's *coding* has landed (BKAAI
wrapped); the prior file-surface-churn concern is now historical — the tree
is settled and the re-validation confirmed the enum-value sites survived
intact.

What remains and matters:
- **Start gate:** ₣BK's two-platform gauntlet acceptance is still pending.
  Begin this heat only after ₣BK's gauntlet passes on linux + macos and any
  cutover bugfixes land — so a known-good cutover baseline exists and every
  ₣BM gauntlet signal is unambiguously about enums, not the cutover.
- **Residual churn risk:** only late ₣BK bugfixes from its gauntlet runs.
  Low; re-grep before editing if BK commits land meanwhile.
- The bubep→bunne harmonize touches BUK files (₣BK's other subtree). ₣BK's
  BUK code has landed, so no hard coordination remains — just awareness of
  any late bugfix churn there.

## What done looks like

- All in-scope bare enum values replaced with sprue-prefixed values across
  bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build), and Rust; `RBRN_RUNTIME` left bare.
- `buv_enum_enroll` (and gated `buv_gate_enroll`) declarations updated to
  expect the new values; regime files (`rbrv.env`, `rbrn.env`, BURN
  profiles) updated in place.
- Class-4 derived strings (pool suffixes, hallmark prefixes) left bare;
  their selecting dispatch migrated.
- `regime-validation` + `dockerfile-hygiene` + `gauntlet` green.
- Vessel-mode additionally proven by a real conjure + graft cloud build.
- A single grep per family returns the complete cluster:
  `grep -rn rbnve_ Tools/`, `grep -rn rbnne_ Tools/`,
  `grep -rn bunne_ Tools/` — and `grep -rn bubep_` returns nothing.

## Out of scope

- Sprue convention applied to non-regime-enum domains (status codes, JSON
  wire-format keys, etc.) — separate heat.
- `RBRN_RUNTIME` (`docker`/`podman`) — executable-name coupling, excluded
  by decision above.
- `BURE_VERBOSE` / `BURE_COLOR` integer values — different problem class.
- Backwards-compatibility shims of any kind.

## Pace shape (deferred to mount-time)

Resolved cadence is per-variable-family, ordered cheap-pilot-first:

1. Convention spec authoring — formalize the convention as a spec section,
   citing `mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay` for grounding, plus the class-4
   "coupled derived string stays bare" rule (two witnesses) and the
   `RBRN_RUNTIME` exclusion rationale.
2. Egress-mode pilot — `rbnve_tether`/`_airgap`. Smallest, cleanest;
   proves the mechanical pattern and the class-4 distinction.
3. Vessel-mode — `rbnve_bind`/`_conjure`/`_graft`. Heaviest; crosses
   bash/adoc/Python(Cloud Build)/Rust. Acceptance gate = real conjure +
   graft cloud build.
4. Node-mode — `rbnne_*` for ENTRY/DNS/ACCESS. High English-word noise,
   heavy classification.
5. bubep → bunne harmonize — BUK files; last slice.
6. Final regression sweep — gauntlet end-to-end + per-family grep audit.

Slate as thin dockets at mount-time (depth in slate commit messages); do
not pre-cut detailed site lists from this paddock.

# jjezs_pace ₢BMAAH

## Character
Test-infrastructure fixture; mechanical once shape is set. A cheap cloud-build
probe — confirms the build/retrieve path yields a runnable artifact, with no
crucible apparatus.

## Goal
Prove the cloud-depot build-and-retrieve path yields a *runnable* artifact:
ordain (conjure-mode) `rbev-busybox` → summon → bare container-runtime run of a
degenerate command (assert exit 0) → abjure. Standing depot, no per-run levy.

## Suite placement — sibling to skirmish, NOT a member of it
This is a new standing-depot scenario-chain suite named `dogfight`, sibling to
`skirmish` in the operator-precondition family (both reuse a standing
operator-levied depot via the `canonical-invest` idiom — no levy, no unmake).
The two differ on the crucible axis and that difference is the whole point:
`skirmish` charges the four crucibles to prove containment; `dogfight` charges
NO crucible and proves only the cloud-build→summon→run path. Do NOT fold this
fixture into the `skirmish` array — that would surround the crucible-free probe
with the exact crucible work it exists to skip. It is emphatically NOT a new
dependency tier "between service and crucible" — the dependency-tier axis
(fast/service/crucible/complete) is a lattice whose top, `complete`, already
means cloud+runtime; this lives on the orthogonal standing-depot scenario axis
where `gauntlet` and `skirmish` already sit.

## Strongest leverage — reuse, don't rebuild
`rbtdrc_hallmark_lifecycle` (rbtdrc_crucible.rs) already does ordain-rbev-busybox
→ … → abjure, including the BURE_CONFIRM-skip abjure. This fixture IS that, with
the registry-inventory middle (audit/rekon) swapped for summon + run. Take the
no-levy standing-depot idiom and the precondition probes from
`RBTDRK_CASES_CANONICAL_INVEST` (rbtdrk_canonical.rs). For the bare run, model on
`rbtdrc_image_layers` — the existing precedent for shelling a raw
container-runtime command from a fixture.

## Locked constraints
- **No crucible.** Proves build/depot/access viability, NOT containment (the
  crucible's orthogonal axis). A bare `<runtime> run --rm <ref> true` suffices —
  no sentry/pentacle/bottle/network. busybox's default cmd is `sh`, so pass an
  explicit `true` to get a clean exit-0 executability proof.
- **Runtime hardcoded to docker behind ONE named constant** (podman deferred).
  The Director-governed runtime-regime-field decision rides with podman, after
  ₣BS settles the regime/credential shape — do NOT add a regime field now.
- Presumes an operator-levied standing depot with director + retriever already
  invested; carry precondition probes like canonical-invest does.

## One unknown to resolve at mount (deliberately not pre-baked)
How `summon` (rbw-fs RetrieverSummonsHallmark) names the locally-pulled image —
the run needs that ref. Read it from the summon impl or its emitted burv fact.

## Suite wiring
Mint a `dogfight` suite paralleling `skirmish` in rbte_engine.sh:
`ZRBTE_SUITE_DOGFIGHT` array, the `dogfight)` resolver case arm, and the
`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.dogfight.sh` tabtarget. A single-fixture suite is fine —
the name is the operator's memorable batch handle. (A `rbw-t?` Qualify wrapper
paralleling QualifySkirmish is optional and deferred — not part of done.)

## Registration
New RBTDRM_FIXTURE_* const + fixtures roster entry + required-colophons map
(must include summon alongside ordain/abjure). StateProgressing disposition —
cases are sequentially dependent (conjure→summon→run→abjure).

## Done
A fixture + single-case run path that ordains busybox, summons it, runs a
degenerate command proving executability, and abjures — green against a standing
depot, no crucible charged, reachable via the new `dogfight` suite tabtarget.

content.text:
Mounted **₣BM** → next actionable pace **₢BMAAH** (cloud-depot-build-viability-fixture) [rough].

## Goal (one sentence)

Prove the cloud-depot build-and-retrieve path yields a *runnable* artifact: ordain a conjure-mode `rbev-busybox` → summon it → bare `<runtime> run --rm <ref> true` (assert exit 0) → abjure — against a standing operator-levied depot, no crucible charged.

## Heat context

**₣BM — rbk-09-mvp-regime-sprues-misc** (8/10 done). The heat's spine was the regime-enum *sprue* migration (bare `conjure`/`tether`/etc. → prefixed `rbnve_*`/`rbnne_*`/`bunne_*`), which has landed. This pace is one of the two remaining and is a **misc test-infrastructure fixture**, orthogonal to the sprue work — it just lives in the same heat's grab-bag.

Recent work: tabtarget dispatch via BURD_LAUNCHER (₢BMAAJ), theurge commit-hygiene guard (₢BMAAI).

## The docket

A new **`dogfight`** suite — a standing-depot scenario chain, **sibling to `skirmish`, not a member of it**. The whole point is the crucible axis: `skirmish` charges four crucibles to prove containment; `dogfight` charges **none** and proves only cloud-build→summon→run. It is *not* a new dependency tier between service and crucible.

Strongest leverage is reuse:
- `rbtdrc_hallmark_lifecycle` (rbtdrc_crucible.rs) already does ordain-busybox → … → abjure. This fixture *is* that, with the audit/rekon middle swapped for **summon + run**.
- No-levy standing-depot idiom + precondition probes from `RBTDRK_CASES_CANONICAL_INVEST` (rbtdrk_canonical.rs).
- Bare-run modeled on `rbtdrc_image_layers` (existing precedent for shelling raw runtime from a fixture).

**Locked constraints:** no crucible; runtime hardcoded to docker behind **one named constant** (podman deferred, no regime field now — that rides with ₣BS); presumes director+retriever already invested.

**One deliberately-unbaked unknown:** how `summon` (rbw-fs) names the locally-pulled image — the run needs that ref. Resolve at mount by reading the summon impl / its emitted burv fact.

**Wiring:** `ZRBTE_SUITE_DOGFIGHT` array + `dogfight)` resolver arm + `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.dogfight.sh` in rbte_engine.sh. **Registration:** new `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` const + roster entry + required-colophons map (must include summon). StateProgressing disposition (sequential: conjure→summon→run→abjure).

## Name assessment

Silks `cloud-depot-build-viability-fixture` is accurate but the docket's own handle is **dogfight**. I'd suggest relabeling to something carrying that word (e.g. `dogfight-cloud-build-viability`) so the suite name and pace name align — but it's optional.

## Proposed approach

1. **Resolve the summon-ref unknown** — read `rbw-fs`/`rbtdrf` summon impl + burv fact to learn the local image ref.
2. **Author the fixture** in a new `rbtdr?_*.

32	    rbtdre_Case, rbtdre_Disposition, rbtdre_Fixture, rbtdre_Verdict,
33	};

36	    rbtdri_parse_ifrit_verdict, rbtdri_read_burv_fact, rbtdri_read_burv_facts_multi,

41	    RBTDRM_COLOPHON_BARK, RBTDRM_COLOPHON_CHARGE, RBTDRM_COLOPHON_CRUCIBLE_ACTIVE,
42	    RBTDRM_COLOPHON_FIAT, RBTDRM_COLOPHON_JETTISON_HALLMARK_IMAGE, RBTDRM_COLOPHON_ORDAIN,
43	    RBTDRM_COLOPHON_QUENCH, RBTDRM_COLOPHON_REKON_HALLMARK, RBTDRM_COLOPHON_TALLY,
44	    RBTDRM_COLOPHON_VOUCH, RBTDRM_COLOPHON_WRIT, RBTDRM_ROLE_DIRECTOR, RBTDRM_ROLE_GOVERNOR,
45	    RBTDRM_ROLE_PAYOR, RBTDRM_ROLE_RETRIEVER,
46	};
47	
48	// ── Thread-local invocation context ──────────────────────────
49	
50	/// Ifrit binary name inside the bottle container.
51	const RBTDRC_IFRIT_BINARY: &str = "rbid";
52	
53	/// Test connectivity target — ICANN-owned, stable single /20 CIDR (192.0.32.0/20)
54	const RBTDRC_CONNECTIVITY_DOMAIN: &str = "www.internic.net";
55	
56	thread_local! {
57	    static RBTDRC_CTX: RefCell<Option<rbtdri_Context>> = RefCell::new(None);
58	}
59	
60	/// Store invocation context for case functions. Called before run_sections.
61	pub fn rbtdrc_set_context(ctx: rbtdri_Context) {
62	    RBTDRC_CTX.with(|c| *c.borrow_mut() = Some(ctx));
63	}
64	
65	/// Retrieve invocation context after cases complete. Called for quench.
66	pub fn rbtdrc_take_context() -> rbtdri_Context {
67	    RBTDRC_CTX.with(|c| {
68	        c.borrow_mut()
69	            .take()
70	            .expect("rbtdrc: no context — was rbtdrc_set_context called?")
71	    })
72	}
73	
74	pub(crate) fn rbtdrc_with_ctx<F>(f: F) -> rbtdre_Verdict
75	where
76	    F: FnOnce(&mut rbtdri_Context) -> rbtdre_Verdict,
77	{
78	    RBTDRC_CTX.with(|c| {
79	        let mut opt = c.borrow_mut();
80	        let ctx = opt
81	            .as_mut()
82	            .expect("rbtdrc: no invocation context for case execution");
83	        f(ctx)
84	    })
85	}
86	
87	// ── Crucible lifecycle hooks (setup/teardown) ────────────────
88	
89	/// Setup hook for crucible fixtures: charge → CrucibleActive assertion.
90	/// Reads ctx from thread-local. Returns Err on any step failure; engine
91	/// surfaces this as a fixture failure and runs teardown anyway.
92	///
93	/// Service-readiness waits are folded into the charge tabtarget itself,
94	/// driven by the per-nameplate `RBRN_BOTTLE_READINESS_DELAY_SEC` fact.
95	pub fn rbtdrc_charge_crucible() -> Result<(), String> {
96	    RBTDRC_CTX.with(|c| {
97	        let mut opt = c.borrow_mut();
98	        let ctx = opt
99	            .as_mut()
100	            .ok_or_else(|| "rbtdrc: no invocation context for charge".to_string())?;
101	        zrbtdrc_charge_impl(ctx)
102	    })
103	}
104	
105	/// Teardown hook for crucible fixtures: quench → inverse CrucibleActive
106	/// assertion. Reads ctx from thread-local. Best-effort — surfaces failures as
107	/// stderr warnings rather than errors; teardown always completes.
108	pub fn rbtdrc_quench_crucible() {
109	    RBTDRC_CTX.with(|c| {
110	        let mut opt = c.borrow_mut();
111	        if let Some(ctx) = opt.as_mut() {
112	            zrbtdrc_quench_impl(ctx);
113	        } else {
114	            crate::rbtdrg_error_now!("rbtdrc: no invocation context for quench — skipped");
115	        }
116	    });
117	}
118	
119	fn zrbtdrc_charge_impl(ctx: &mut rbtdri_Context) -> Result<(), String> {
120	    let fixture = ctx.fixture().to_string();
121	    crate::rbtdrg_info_now!("Charging crucible for nameplate '{}'...", fixture);
122	    match rbtdri_invoke(ctx, RBTDRM_COLOPHON_CHARGE, &[]) {
123	        Ok(r) if r.exit_code == 0 => {
124	            crate::rbtdrg_info_now!("Crucible charged");
125	        }
126	        Ok(r) => {
127	            return Err(format!("Charge failed (exit {})\n{}", r.exit_code, r.stderr));
128	        }
129	        Err(e) => {
130	            return Err(format!("Charge invocation failed: {}", e));
131	        }
132	    }
133	
134	    crate::rbtdrg_info_now!("Verifying crucible is active after charge...");
135	    match rbtdri_invoke_global(ctx, RBTDRM_COLOPHON_CRUCIBLE_ACTIVE, &[&fixture], &[]) {
136	        Ok(r) if r.exit_code == 0 => {
137	            crate::rbtdrg_info_now!("Crucible active confirmed");
138	        }
139	        Ok(r) => {
140	            return Err(format!(
141	                "Lifecycle assertion failed: crucible not active after charge (exit {})\n{}",
142	                r.exit_code, r.stderr
143	            ));
144	        }
145	        Err(e) => {
146	            return Err(format!(
147	                "CrucibleActive invocation failed after charge: {}",
148	                e
149	            ));
150	        }
151	    }
152	
153	    Ok(())
154	}

187	/// Invoke ifrit inside the bottle via bark, saving stdout/stderr to case dir.
188	fn rbtdrc_invoke_ifrit(
189	    ctx: &mut rbtdri_Context,
190	    attack: &str,
191	    dir: &Path,
192	) -> rbtdre_Verdict {
193	    let result = match rbtdri_invoke(ctx, RBTDRM_COLOPHON_BARK, &[RBTDRC_IFRIT_BINARY, attack]) {
194	        Ok(r) => r,
195	        Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("bark invocation error: {}", e)),
196	    };
197	    let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("bark-stdout.txt"), &result.stdout);
198	    let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("bark-stderr.txt"), &result.stderr);
199	    rbtdri_parse_ifrit_verdict(&result.stdout, result.exit_code)
200	}
201	
202	/// Invoke ifrit inside the bottle via bark with extra arguments.
203	fn rbtdrc_invoke_ifrit_with_args(
204	    ctx: &mut rbtdri_Context,
205	    attack: &str,
206	    extra_args: &[&str],
207	    dir: &Path,
208	) -> rbtdre_Verdict {
209	    let mut bark_args = vec![RBTDRC_IFRIT_BINARY, attack];
210	    bark_args.extend_from_slice(extra_args);
211	    let result = match rbtdri_invoke(ctx, RBTDRM_COLOPHON_BARK, &bark_args) {
212	        Ok(r) => r,
213	        Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("bark invocation error: {}", e)),
214	    };
215	    let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("bark-stdout.txt"), &result.stdout);
216	    let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("bark-stderr.txt"), &result.stderr);
217	    rbtdri_parse_ifrit_verdict(&result.stdout, result.exit_code)
218	}

220	/// Execute a command in the sentry via writ, returning captured stdout.
221	fn rbtdrc_writ(ctx: &mut rbtdri_Context, args: &[&str]) -> Result<String, String> {
222	    let result = rbtdri_invoke(ctx, RBTDRM_COLOPHON_WRIT, args)?;
223	    if result.exit_code != 0 {
224	        return Err(format!(
225	            "writ exit {}\nstdout: {}\nstderr: {}",
226	            result.exit_code, result.stdout, result.stderr
227	        ));
228	    }
229	    Ok(result.stdout)
230	}

232	/// Execute a command in the pentacle via fiat, returning the invocation result.
233	fn rbtdrc_fiat(ctx: &mut rbtdri_Context, args: &[&str]) -> Result<String, String> {
234	    let result = rbtdri_invoke(ctx, RBTDRM_COLOPHON_FIAT, args)?;
235	    if result.exit_code != 0 {
236	        return Err(format!(
237	            "fiat exit {}\nstdout: {}\nstderr: {}",
238	            result.exit_code, result.stdout, result.stderr
239	        ));
240	    }
241	    Ok(result.stdout)
242	}

244	/// Discover the sentry's enclave IP by reading /etc/resolv.conf from the pentacle via fiat.
245	/// The pentacle uses the sentry as its DNS server, so resolv.conf nameserver = sentry enclave IP.
246	/// (Sentry's own resolv.conf points to upstream DNS like 8.8.8.8 — wrong for enclave ops.)
247	fn rbtdrc_discover_sentry_ip(ctx: &mut rbtdri_Context) -> Result<String, String> {
248	    let output = rbtdrc_fiat(ctx, &["cat", "/etc/resolv.conf"])?;
249	    for line in output.lines() {
250	        let trimmed = line.trim();
251	        if let Some(rest) = trimmed.strip_prefix("nameserver") {
252	            let ip = rest.trim();
253	            if !ip.is_empty() && rbtdrc_looks_like_ip(ip) {
254	                return Ok(ip.to_string());
255	            }
256	        }
257	    }
258	    Err(format!(
259	        "no nameserver found in pentacle /etc/resolv.conf:\n{}",
260	        output
261	    ))
262	}
263	
264	/// Resolve a hostname via writ (running dig +short on the sentry).
265	/// Returns the first line that looks like an IP address (filters BUK log headers).
266	fn rbtdrc_resolve_via_writ(ctx: &mut rbtdri_Context, hostname: &str) -> Result<String, String> {
267	    let output = rbtdrc_writ(ctx, &["dig", "+short", hostname])?;
268	    let ip = output
269	        .lines()
270	        .find(|line| {
271	            let t = line.trim();
272	            !t.is_empty() && rbtdrc_looks_like_ip(t)
273	        })
274	        .map(|line| line.trim().to_string())
275	        .ok_or_else(|| format!("dig +short {} returned no IP:\n{}", hostname, output))?;
276	    Ok(ip)
277	}
278	
279	/// Quick check: does this string look like an IPv4 address (digits and dots only)?
280	/// Not a full validator — just enough to reject BUK log headers and DNS comments.
281	fn rbtdrc_looks_like_ip(s: &str) -> bool {
282	    !s.is_empty() && s.chars().all(|c| c.is_ascii_digit() || c == '.')
283	}
284	
285	/// Extract iptables rule lines from writ output, filtering BUK log headers.
286	/// Rules start with -P (policy), -N (new chain), or -A (append).
287	fn rbtdrc_extract_iptables_rules(output: &str) -> String {
288	    output
289	        .lines()
290	        .filter(|l| {
291	            let t = l.trim();
292	            t.starts_with("-P ") || t.starts_with("-N ") || t.starts_with("-A ")
293	        })
294	        .collect::<Vec<_>>()
295	        .join("\n")
296	}
297	
298	/// Filter writ output to remove BUK log headers (lines starting with known prefixes).
299	/// Retains only the actual command output.
300	fn rbtdrc_filter_writ_output(output: &str) -> String {
301	    output
302	        .lines()
303	        .filter(|l| {
304	            let t = l.trim();
305	            !t.starts_with("log files:")
306	                && !t.starts_with("transcript:")
307	                && !t.starts_with("output dir:")
308	                && !t.starts_with("\u{1b}[90m") // ANSI escape for BUK colored prefix
309	        })
310	        .collect::<Vec<_>>()
311	        .join("\n")
312	}
313	
314	/// Read a named environment variable from sentry's process environment via writ.
315	fn rbtdrc_read_sentry_env(ctx: &mut rbtdri_Context, var: &str) -> Result<String, String> {
316	    let output = rbtdrc_writ(ctx, &["printenv", var])?;
317	    rbtdrc_filter_writ_output(&output)
318	        .lines()
319	        .map(|l| l.trim())
320	        .find(|l| !l.is_empty())
321	        .map(|l| l.to_string())
322	        .ok_or_else(|| format!("printenv {} returned no value", var))
323	}

327	fn rbtdrc_pentacle_dnsmasq_responds(dir: &Path) -> rbtdre_Verdict {
328	    rbtdrc_with_ctx(|ctx| {
329	        let sentry_ip = match rbtdrc_discover_sentry_ip(ctx) {
330	            Ok(ip) => ip,
331	            Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("sentry IP discovery: {}", e)),
332	        };
333	        let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("sentry-ip.txt"), &sentry_ip);
334	
335	        let output = match rbtdrc_fiat(
336	            ctx,
337	            &["dig", "+short", &format!("@{}", sentry_ip), RBTDRC_CONNECTIVITY_DOMAIN],
338	        ) {
339	            Ok(o) => o,
340	            Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("fiat dig error: {}", e)),
341	        };
342	        let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("fiat-stdout.txt"), &output);
343	
344	        if output.trim().is_empty() {
345	            return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!(
346	                "dnsmasq on sentry {} returned empty response for {}",
347	                sentry_ip, RBTDRC_CONNECTIVITY_DOMAIN
348	            ));
349	        }
350	        rbtdre_Verdict::Pass
351	    })
352	}

440	fn rbtdrc_sentry_iptables_loaded(dir: &Path) -> rbtdre_Verdict {
441	    rbtdrc_with_ctx(|ctx| {
442	        let output = match rbtdrc_writ(ctx, &["iptables", "-S"]) {
443	            Ok(o) => o,
444	            Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("writ error: {}", e)),
445	        };
446	        let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("iptables-rules.txt"), &output);
447	
448	        if output.trim().is_empty() {
449	            return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail("iptables -S returned empty output".to_string());
450	        }
451	        // Expect at least one policy line (-P) and one append rule (-A)
452	        if !output.contains("-P") || !output.contains("-A") {
453	            return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!(
454	                "iptables rules incomplete (missing -P or -A):\n{}",
455	                output
456	            ));
457	        }
458	        rbtdre_Verdict::Pass
459	    })
460	}

462	// Structural-presence backstops for the load-bearing iptables clauses and
463	// kernel sysctl set by rbjs_sentry.sh. Each case is a fast read-and-check
464	// against sentry's runtime state — fail loudly if a future change strips a
465	// defensive clause the architecture depends on.
466	
467	fn rbtdrc_sentry_config_rp_filter(dir: &Path) -> rbtdre_Verdict {
468	    rbtdrc_with_ctx(|ctx| {
469	        let entry_mode = match rbtdrc_read_sentry_env(ctx, "RBRN_ENTRY_MODE") {
470	            Ok(m) => m,
471	            Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("read RBRN_ENTRY_MODE: {}", e)),
472	        };
473	        let output = match rbtdrc_writ(ctx, &["cat", "/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/all/rp_filter"]) {
474	            Ok(o) => o,
475	            Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("writ error: {}", e)),
476	        };
477	        let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("rp_filter.txt"), &output);
478	
479	        let value = rbtdrc_filter_writ_output(&output)
480	            .lines()
481	            .map(|l| l.trim())
482	            .find(|l| !l.is_empty())
483	            .map(|l| l.to_string())
484	            .unwrap_or_default();
485	
486	        let expected = match entry_mode.as_str() {
487	            "rbnne_enabled" => "2",
488	            "rbnne_disabled" => "1",
489	            other => {
490	                return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("unexpected RBRN_ENTRY_MODE '{}'", other));
491	            }
492	        };
493	
494	        if value != expected {
495	            return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!(
496	                "rp_filter = {} (expected {} for entry-mode {})",
497	                value, expected, entry_mode
498	            ));
499	        }
500	        rbtdre_Verdict::Pass
501	    })
502	}
503	
504	fn rbtdrc_sentry_config_prerouting_dnat(dir: &Path) -> rbtdre_Verdict {
505	    rbtdrc_with_ctx(|ctx| {
506	        let entry_mode = match rbtdrc_read_sentry_env(ctx, "RBRN_ENTRY_MODE") {
507	            Ok(m) => m,
508	            Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("read RBRN_ENTRY_MODE: {}", e)),
509	        };
510	        if entry_mode != "rbnne_enabled" {
511	            return rbtdre_Verdict::Skip(format!("entry-mode={}", entry_mode));
512	        }
513	        let output = match rbtdrc_writ(ctx, &["iptables", "-t", "nat", "-S", "PREROUTING"]) {
514	            Ok(o) => o,
515	            Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("writ error: {}", e)),
516	        };
517	        let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("prerouting.txt"), &output);
518	
519	        let append_rules: Vec<&str> = output
520	            .lines()
521	            .filter(|l| l.trim().starts_with("-A "))
522	            .collect();
523	
524	        let return_positions: Vec<usize> = append_rules
525	            .iter()
526	            .enumerate()
527	            .filter(|(_, r)| r.contains("-j RETURN"))
528	            .map(|(i, _)| i)
529	            .collect();
530	        let dnat_positions: Vec<usize> = append_rules
531	            .iter()
532	            .enumerate()
533	            .filter(|(_, r)| r.contains("-j DNAT"))
534	            .map(|(i, _)| i)
535	            .collect();
536	
537	        if return_positions.len() != 2 {
538	            return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!(
539	                "PREROUTING RETURN rule count = {} (expected 2)",
540	                return_positions.len()
541	            ));
542	        }
543	        if dnat_positions.is_empty() {
544	            return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail("PREROUTING DNAT rule absent".to_string());
545	        }
546	        let max_return = *return_positions.iter().max().unwrap();
547	        let min_dnat = *dnat_positions.iter().min().unwrap();
548	        if max_return >= min_dnat {
549	            return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!(
550	                "PREROUTING RETURN at position {} not before DNAT at position {}",
551	                max_return, min_dnat
552	            ));
553	        }
554	        rbtdre_Verdict::Pass
555	    })
556	}
557	
558	fn rbtdrc_sentry_config_postrouting_masquerade(dir: &Path) -> rbtdre_Verdict {
559	    rbtdrc_with_ctx(|ctx| {
560	        let entry_mode = match rbtdrc_read_sentry_env(ctx, "RBRN_ENTRY_MODE") {
561	            Ok(m) => m,
562	            Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("read RBRN_ENTRY_MODE: {}", e)),
563	        };
564	        if entry_mode != "rbnne_enabled" {
565	            return rbtdre_Verdict::Skip(format!("entry-mode={}", entry_mode));
566	        }
567	        let output = match rbtdrc_writ(ctx, &["iptables", "-t", "nat", "-S", "POSTROUTING"]) {
568	            Ok(o) => o,
569	            Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("writ error: {}", e)),
570	        };
571	        let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("postrouting.txt"), &output);
572	
573	        // Entry-port MASQUERADE has -p tcp and --dport; the egress MASQUERADE
574	        // (uplink path, set when uplink access-mode != disabled) has neither.
575	        let entry_masq = output
576	            .lines()
577	            .any(|l| l.contains("-j MASQUERADE") && l.contains("-p tcp") && l.contains("--dport"));
578	
579	        if !entry_masq {
580	            return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(
581	                "POSTROUTING entry-port MASQUERADE rule absent".to_string(),
582	            );
583	        }
584	        rbtdre_Verdict::Pass
585	    })
586	}
587	
588	fn rbtdrc_sentry_config_forward_estab_related(dir: &Path) -> rbtdre_Verdict {
589	    rbtdrc_with_ctx(|ctx| {
590	        let output = match rbtdrc_writ(ctx, &["iptables", "-S", "FORWARD"]) {
591	            Ok(o) => o,
592	            Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("writ error: {}", e)),
593	        };
594	        let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("forward.txt"), &output);
595	
596	        let append_rules: Vec<&str> = output
597	            .lines()
598	            .filter(|l| l.trim().starts_with("-A "))
599	            .collect();
600	
601	        let estab_pos = append_rules
602	            .iter()
603	            .position(|r| r.contains("RELATED") && r.contains("ESTABLISHED") && r.contains("-j ACCEPT"));
604	        let jump_pos = append_rules
605	            .iter()
606	            .position(|r| r.contains("-j RBM-FORWARD"));
607	
608	        let estab_pos = match estab_pos {
609	            Some(p) => p,
610	            None => {
611	                return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(
612	                    "FORWARD ESTABLISHED,RELATED ACCEPT rule absent".to_string(),
613	                );
614	            }
615	        };
616	        let jump_pos = match jump_pos {
617	            Some(p) => p,
618	            None => {
619	                return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail("FORWARD -j RBM-FORWARD jump absent".to_string());
620	            }
621	        };
622	
623	        if estab_pos >= jump_pos {
624	            return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!(
625	                "FORWARD ESTABLISHED,RELATED at position {} not before RBM-FORWARD jump at position {}",
626	                estab_pos, jump_pos
627	            ));
628	        }
629	        rbtdre_Verdict::Pass
630	    })
631	}

633	fn rbtdrc_dns_blocked_with_observation(dir: &Path) -> rbtdre_Verdict {
634	    rbtdrc_with_ctx(|ctx| {
635	        // Before: capture iptables state from sentry
636	        let before = match rbtdrc_writ(ctx, &["iptables", "-L", "-v", "-n", "-x"]) {
637	            Ok(o) => {
638	                let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("iptables-before.txt"), &o);
639	                o
640	            }
641	            Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("before-capture: {}", e)),
642	        };
643	
644	        // Attack: invoke ifrit dns-blocked-google inside bottle
645	        let ifrit_verdict = rbtdrc_invoke_ifrit(ctx, "dns-blocked-google", dir);
646	
647	        // After: capture iptables state from sentry
648	        let after = match rbtdrc_writ(ctx, &["iptables", "-L", "-v", "-n", "-x"]) {
649	            Ok(o) => {
650	                let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("iptables-after.txt"), &o);
651	                o
652	            }
653	            Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("after-capture: {}", e)),
654	        };
655	
656	        // Log observation: did sentry state change during the attack?
657	        let delta = if before != after {
658	            "CHANGED — sentry processed traffic during attack"
659	        } else {
660	            "UNCHANGED — no observable sentry state change"
661	        };
662	        let observation = format!(
663	            "Ifrit verdict: {}\nIptables delta: {}\n",
664	            match &ifrit_verdict {
665	                rbtdre_Verdict::Pass => "PASS",
666	                rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(_) => "FAIL",
667	                rbtdre_Verdict::Skip(_) => "SKIP",
668	            },
669	            delta,
670	        );
671	        let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("observation.txt"), &observation);
672	
673	        // Primary verdict is the ifrit result
674	        ifrit_verdict
675	    })
676	}
