811	/// Coordinated: attempt outbound connections from sentry itself to non-allowed destinations.
812	/// The sentry's OUTPUT DROP policy should block these — verifies sentry can't be used as a pivot.
813	fn rbtdrc_coordinated_sentry_egress_lockdown(dir: &Path) -> rbtdre_Verdict {
814	    rbtdrc_with_ctx(|ctx| {
815	        // Negative: attempt TCP to 1.1.1.1:443 from sentry via bash /dev/tcp
816	        let tcp1_result = rbtdri_invoke(
817	            ctx,
818	            RBTDRM_COLOPHON_WRIT,
819	            &["timeout", "2", "bash", "-c", "echo > /dev/tcp/1.1.1.1/443"],
820	        );
821	        let tcp1_blocked = match &tcp1_result {
822	            Ok(r) => {
823	                let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("tcp1-stdout.txt"), &r.stdout);
824	                let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("tcp1-stderr.txt"), &r.stderr);
825	                r.exit_code != 0
826	            }
827	            Err(e) => {
828	                let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("tcp1-error.txt"), format!("{}", e));
829	                true // invocation error = blocked
830	            }
831	        };
832	        if !tcp1_blocked {
833	            return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(
834	                "BREACH: sentry TCP to 1.1.1.1:443 succeeded — OUTPUT chain not blocking egress"
835	                    .to_string(),
836	            );
837	        }
838	
839	        // Negative: attempt TCP to 140.82.121.4:443 (GitHub) from sentry
840	        let tcp2_result = rbtdri_invoke(
841	            ctx,
842	            RBTDRM_COLOPHON_WRIT,
843	            &[
844	                "timeout",
845	                "2",
846	                "bash",
847	                "-c",
848	                "echo > /dev/tcp/140.82.121.4/443",
849	            ],
850	        );
851	        let tcp2_blocked = match &tcp2_result {
852	            Ok(r) => {
853	                let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("tcp2-stdout.txt"), &r.stdout);
854	                let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("tcp2-stderr.txt"), &r.stderr);
855	                r.exit_code != 0
856	            }
857	            Err(e) => {
858	                let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("tcp2-error.txt"), format!("{}", e));
859	                true
860	            }
861	        };
862	        if !tcp2_blocked {
863	            return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(
864	                "BREACH: sentry TCP to 140.82.121.4:443 succeeded — OUTPUT chain not blocking egress"
865	                    .to_string(),
866	            );
867	        }
868	
869	        // Positive control: dig @8.8.8.8 connectivity domain from sentry must succeed
870	        // (proves dnsmasq's egress path works — specific OUTPUT ACCEPT for DNS)
871	        let dig_result = match rbtdrc_writ(
872	            ctx,
873	            &["dig", "+short", "@8.8.8.8", RBTDRC_CONNECTIVITY_DOMAIN],
874	        ) {
875	            Ok(o) => o,
876	            Err(e) => {
877	                return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!(
878	                    "positive control failed: dig @8.8.8.8 {} from sentry: {}", RBTDRC_CONNECTIVITY_DOMAIN,
879	                    e
880	                ))
881	            }
882	        };
883	        let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("dig-positive.txt"), &dig_result);
884	
885	        let has_ip = dig_result
886	            .lines()
887	            .any(|l| rbtdrc_looks_like_ip(l.trim()));
888	        if !has_ip {
889	            return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!(
890	                "positive control: dig @8.8.8.8 {} returned no IP:\n{}", RBTDRC_CONNECTIVITY_DOMAIN,
891	                dig_result
892	            ));
893	        }
894	
895	        let _ = std::fs::write(
896	            dir.join("observation.txt"),
897	            "Sentry egress lockdown verified: TCP to 1.1.1.1:443 blocked, TCP to 140.82.121.4:443 blocked, DNS egress works",
898	        );
899	        rbtdre_Verdict::Pass
900	    })
901	}

903	/// Coordinated: invoke ifrit DNS queries (allowed + blocked), then read dnsmasq log
904	/// to verify both queries appear. Proves audit trail is intact.
905	fn rbtdrc_coordinated_dnsmasq_query_audit(dir: &Path) -> rbtdre_Verdict {
906	    rbtdrc_with_ctx(|ctx| {
907	        // Invoke ifrit dns-allowed-example via bark
908	        let allowed_result = match rbtdri_invoke(
909	            ctx,
910	            RBTDRM_COLOPHON_BARK,
911	            &[RBTDRC_IFRIT_BINARY, "dns-allowed-example"],
912	        ) {
913	            Ok(r) => r,
914	            Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("bark dns-allowed-example: {}", e)),
915	        };
916	        let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("allowed-stdout.txt"), &allowed_result.stdout);
917	        let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("allowed-stderr.txt"), &allowed_result.stderr);
918	
919	        // Invoke ifrit dns-blocked-google via bark
920	        let blocked_result = match rbtdri_invoke(
921	            ctx,
922	            RBTDRM_COLOPHON_BARK,
923	            &[RBTDRC_IFRIT_BINARY, "dns-blocked-google"],
924	        ) {
925	            Ok(r) => r,
926	            Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("bark dns-blocked-google: {}", e)),
927	        };
928	        let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("blocked-stdout.txt"), &blocked_result.stdout);
929	        let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("blocked-stderr.txt"), &blocked_result.stderr);
930	
931	        // Brief delay for dnsmasq log flush
932	        std::thread::sleep(std::time::Duration::from_millis(500));
933	
934	        // Read dnsmasq log from sentry via writ
935	        let log_output = match rbtdrc_writ(ctx, &["cat", "/var/log/dnsmasq.log"]) {
936	            Ok(o) => o,
937	            Err(e) => {
938	                return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!(
939	                    "cannot read dnsmasq log: {}",
940	                    e
941	                ))
942	            }
943	        };
944	        let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("dnsmasq-log.txt"), &log_output);
945	
946	        // Filter to actual log lines (strip BUK headers)
947	        let log_content = rbtdrc_filter_writ_output(&log_output);
948	
949	        // Verify connectivity domain query appears in log
950	        let has_example = log_content
951	            .lines()
952	            .any(|l| l.contains(RBTDRC_CONNECTIVITY_DOMAIN));
953	        if !has_example {
954	            return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(
955	                format!("dnsmasq log missing {} query — audit trail incomplete", RBTDRC_CONNECTIVITY_DOMAIN),
956	            );
957	        }
958	
959	        // Verify google.com query appears in log (blocked queries should still be logged)
960	        let has_google = log_content
961	            .lines()
962	            .any(|l| l.contains("google.com"));
963	        if !has_google {
964	            return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(
965	                "dnsmasq log missing google.com query — blocked queries not audited".to_string(),
966	            );
967	        }
968	
969	        let _ = std::fs::write(
970	            dir.join("observation.txt"),
971	            format!("dnsmasq audit verified: both allowed ({}) and blocked (google.com) queries logged", RBTDRC_CONNECTIVITY_DOMAIN),
972	        );
973	        rbtdre_Verdict::Pass
974	    })
975	}

977	/// Coordinated: ifrit sends TCP RST packets at sentry DNS, theurge verifies DNS still works.
978	fn rbtdrc_coordinated_tcp_rst_hijack(dir: &Path) -> rbtdre_Verdict {
979	    rbtdrc_with_ctx(|ctx| {
980	        let sentry_ip = match rbtdrc_discover_sentry_ip(ctx) {
981	            Ok(ip) => ip,
982	            Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("sentry IP discovery: {}", e)),
983	        };
984	        let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("sentry-ip.txt"), &sentry_ip);
985	        let dig_server = format!("@{}", sentry_ip);
986	
987	        // Pre-snapshot: verify DNS works on sentry before attack
988	        let pre_resolve = match rbtdrc_writ(
989	            ctx,
990	            &["dig", "+short", &dig_server, RBTDRC_CONNECTIVITY_DOMAIN],
991	        ) {
992	            Ok(o) => o,
993	            Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("pre dig {}: {}", RBTDRC_CONNECTIVITY_DOMAIN, e)),
994	        };
995	        let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("pre-resolve.txt"), &pre_resolve);
996	
997	        let pre_ip = pre_resolve
998	            .lines()
999	            .find(|l| rbtdrc_looks_like_ip(l.trim()))
1000	            .unwrap_or("")
1001	            .trim()
1002	            .to_string();
1003	        if pre_ip.is_empty() {
1004	            return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(
1005	                format!("pre-snapshot: {} did not resolve — DNS not working before attack", RBTDRC_CONNECTIVITY_DOMAIN),
1006	            );
1007	        }
1008	
1009	        // Attack: invoke tcp-rst-hijack from bottle
1010	        let result = match rbtdri_invoke(
1011	            ctx,
1012	            RBTDRM_COLOPHON_BARK,
1013	            &[RBTDRC_IFRIT_BINARY, "tcp-rst-hijack"],
1014	        ) {
1015	            Ok(r) => r,
1016	            Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("bark invocation: {}", e)),
1017	        };
1018	        let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("bark-stdout.txt"), &result.stdout);
1019	        let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("bark-stderr.txt"), &result.stderr);
1020	
1021	        // Brief pause for any RST effects to propagate
1022	        std::thread::sleep(std::time::Duration::from_millis(500));
1023	
1024	        // Post-snapshot: verify DNS still works on sentry after attack
1025	        let post_resolve = match rbtdrc_writ(
1026	            ctx,
1027	            &["dig", "+short", &dig_server, RBTDRC_CONNECTIVITY_DOMAIN],
1028	        ) {
1029	            Ok(o) => o,
1030	            Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("post dig {}: {}", RBTDRC_CONNECTIVITY_DOMAIN, e)),
1031	        };
1032	        let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("post-resolve.txt"), &post_resolve);
1033	
1034	        let post_ip = post_resolve
1035	            .lines()
1036	            .find(|l| rbtdrc_looks_like_ip(l.trim()))
1037	            .unwrap_or("")
1038	            .trim()
1039	            .to_string();
1040	
1041	        if post_ip.is_empty() {
1042	            return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(
1043	                "BREACH: DNS stopped resolving after TCP RST attack — sentry DNS connection disrupted".to_string(),
1044	            );
1045	        }
1046	
1047	        if pre_ip != post_ip {
1048	            return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!(
1049	                "BREACH: DNS resolution changed after RST attack: {} → {}",
1050	                pre_ip, post_ip
1051	            ));
1052	        }
1053	
1054	        let _ = std::fs::write(
1055	            dir.join("observation.txt"),
1056	            format!(
1057	                "TCP RST hijack defense verified:\n  {}: {} → {} (stable)\n  ifrit exit: {}\n  ifrit output: {}\n",
1058	                RBTDRC_CONNECTIVITY_DOMAIN,
1059	                pre_ip, post_ip, result.exit_code, result.stdout.trim()
1060	            ),
1061	        );
1062	        rbtdre_Verdict::Pass
1063	    })
1064	}

1066	// ── Coordinated attack cases (writ observes sentry, bark attacks) ──
1067	
1068	/// Parse `ip neigh show` output into (IP, MAC) pairs.
1069	/// Format: "10.242.0.3 dev eth1 lladdr 02:42:0a:f2:00:03 REACHABLE"
1070	fn rbtdrc_parse_arp_table(output: &str) -> Vec<(String, String)> {
1071	    let mut entries = Vec::new();
1072	    for line in output.lines() {
1073	        let parts: Vec<&str> = line.split_whitespace().collect();
1074	        // Look for lines with "lladdr" field
1075	        if let Some(pos) = parts.iter().position(|&p| p == "lladdr") {
1076	            if let (Some(&ip), Some(&mac)) = (parts.first(), parts.get(pos + 1)) {
1077	                entries.push((ip.to_string(), mac.to_lowercase()));
1078	            }
1079	        }
1080	    }
1081	    entries
1082	}
1083	
1084	/// Find the MAC associated with a given IP in parsed ARP entries.
1085	fn rbtdrc_arp_mac_for_ip<'a>(entries: &'a [(String, String)], ip: &str) -> Option<&'a str> {
1086	    entries
1087	        .iter()
1088	        .find(|(entry_ip, _)| entry_ip == ip)
1089	        .map(|(_, mac)| mac.as_str())
1090	}
1091

1107	        };

1118	        };
1119	        let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("bark-stdout.txt"), &result.stdout);
1120	        let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join("bark-stderr.txt"), &result.stderr);
1121	
1122	        // If ifrit couldn't execute (AF_PACKET blocked), that's SECURE
1123	        if result.exit_code != 0 {
1124	            let _ = std::fs::write(
1125	                dir.join("observation.txt"),

1127	            );
1128	            return rbtdre_Verdict::Pass;
1129	        }

1131	        // Brief delay for ARP to propagate

1136	            Ok(o) => o,
1137	            Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("post-snapshot: {}", e)),
1138	        };

300	fn rbtdro_ordain_capture_full(
301	    ctx: &mut rbtdri_Context,
302	    dir: &Path,
303	    vessel_dir: &str,
304	    extra_env: &[(&str, &str)],

306	) -> Result<(String, String, String), rbtdre_Verdict> {
307	    let result = rbtdro_invoke_or_fail(

339	/// Yoke the reliquary stamp into every vessel's rbrv.env. The yoke tabtarget
340	/// validates the stamp once against GAR, then wildcard-iterates every vessel
341	/// under ${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}. The orchestrator commits the resulting rbrv.env
342	/// changes after this primitive returns.
343	fn rbtdro_yoke(
344	    ctx: &mut rbtdri_Context,
345	    dir: &Path,
346	    stamp: &str,
347	    label: &str,
348	) -> Result<(), rbtdre_Verdict> {
349	    rbtdro_invoke_or_fail(
350	        ctx,
351	        RBTDRM_OPERATION_YOKE,
352	        "",
353	        RBTDRM_COLOPHON_YOKE_RELIQUARY,
354	        &[stamp],
355	        &[],
356	        dir,
357	        label,
358	    )?;
359	    Ok(())
360	}
361	
362	/// Enshrine one vessel — mirrors that vessel's RBRV_IMAGE_n_ORIGIN bases
363	/// to GAR and writes back the resulting RBRV_IMAGE_n_ANCHOR digests.
364	/// `rbfd_enshrine` is per-vessel; the airgap chain calls this twice with
365	/// different vessels (forge first, then airgap-bottle).
366	fn rbtdro_enshrine(
367	    ctx: &mut rbtdri_Context,
368	    dir: &Path,
369	    vessel_sigil: &str,
370	    label: &str,
371	) -> Result<(), rbtdre_Verdict> {
372	    rbtdro_invoke_or_fail(
373	        ctx,
374	        RBTDRM_OPERATION_ENSHRINE,
375	        vessel_sigil,
376	        RBTDRM_COLOPHON_ENSHRINE_VESSEL,
377	        &[vessel_sigil],
378	        &[],
379	        dir,
380	        label,
381	    )?;
382	    Ok(())
383	}
384	
385	/// Pull a docker image. Used by the graft case to ensure the source image
386	/// is locally resolvable before the BURE_TWEAK-overridden ordain.
387	fn rbtdro_docker_pull(image_ref: &str) -> Result<(), String> {
388	    let output = std::process::Command::new("docker")
389	        .args(["pull", image_ref])
390	        .output()
391	        .map_err(|e| format!("docker pull exec failed: {}", e))?;
392	    if !output.status.success() {
393	        return Err(format!(
394	            "docker pull {} exited {}: {}",
395	            image_ref,
396	            output.status.code().unwrap_or(-1),
397	            String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stderr).trim()
398	        ));
399	    }

#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# RBTE Engine - Theurge test engine implementation module
#
# BCG module providing kindle/sentinel/public functions for theurge
# Rust build, test, and orchestration commands.

set -euo pipefail

# Multiple inclusion guard
test -z "${ZRBTE_SOURCED:-}" || return 0
ZRBTE_SOURCED=1

######################################################################
# Kindle

zrbte_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBTE_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "rbte already kindled"

  local z_dir="${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}"

  readonly RBTE_MANIFEST="${z_dir}/Cargo.toml"
  readonly ZRBTE_BINARY="${z_dir}/target/debug/rbtd"

  # Suite-to-fixture mappings
  ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "handbook-render"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
  )
  ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "hallmark-lifecycle" "batch-vouch")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "hallmark-lifecycle" "batch-vouch" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  # Gauntlet — release-qualification ladder, walks marshal-zero state through
  # canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. Pristine-lifecycle
  # case 1 is the entry-contract gate; preceding enrollment-validation runs
  # state-indifferent and is harmless on broken state. Fail-fast across
  # fixtures is provided by the for-loop's set -e.
  ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "pristine-lifecycle"
    "canonical-establish"
    "onboarding-sequence"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
    "hallmark-lifecycle"
    "tadmor"
    "moriah"
    "srjcl"
    "pluml"
  )
  # Skirmish — the "mini gauntlet": the depot->build->crucible chain WITHOUT
  # project-ID churn. canonical-invest reuses a standing operator-levied depot
  # (no levy, no unmake) where the gauntlet's pristine-lifecycle/canonical
  # -establish each levy a fresh project; pristine-lifecycle is dropped
  # entirely. onboarding-sequence then builds the crucible images (local kludge
  # + cloud ordain into the standing depot) and the four crucibles charge+run.
  # OPERATOR PRECONDITION: a canonical depot already levied — install canonical
  # prefixes and run rbw-dL by hand before this suite. Spends cloud build/GAR
  # but creates no GCP project per run.
  ZRBTE_SUITE_SKIRMISH=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "canonical-invest"
    "onboarding-sequence"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
    "tadmor"
    "moriah"
    "srjcl"
    "pluml"
  )

  readonly ZRBTE_KINDLED=1
}

######################################################################
# Sentinel

zrbte_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBTE_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbte not kindled - call zrbte_kindle first"
}

######################################################################
# Internal helpers

zrbte_build_binary() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  buc_step "Building theurge"
  cargo build --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" || buc_die "cargo build failed"
  test -x "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" || buc_die "Theurge binary not found: ${ZRBTE_BINARY}"
}

zrbte_resolve_suite() {
  local z_suite="$1"
  case "${z_suite}" in
    fast)     echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[*]}" ;;
    service)  echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE[*]}" ;;
    crucible) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE[*]}" ;;
    complete) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE[*]}" ;;
    gauntlet) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET[*]}" ;;
    skirmish) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_SKIRMISH[*]}" ;;
    *)        buc_die "Unknown suite: ${z_suite} (expected fast|service|crucible|complete|gauntlet|skirmish)" ;;
  esac
}

######################################################################
# Public functions

rbte_build() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  buc_step "Building theurge"
  buc_log_args "Manifest: ${RBTE_MANIFEST}"
  cargo build --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" "$@" || buc_die "cargo build failed"
  buc_success "Theurge built"
}

rbte_test() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  buc_step "Testing theurge"
  buc_log_args "Manifest: ${RBTE_MANIFEST}"
  cargo test --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" "$@" || buc_die "cargo test failed"
  buc_success "All theurge tests passed"
}

rbte_run() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  local z_fixture="${BURD_TOKEN_3:-}"
  test -n "${z_fixture}" || buc_die "No fixture imprint — use tabtarget with imprint (e.g. rbtd-r.FixtureRun.tadmor.sh)"

  zrbte_build_binary

  buc_step "Running theurge fixture '${z_fixture}'"
  "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" "${ZRBZ_COLOPHON_MANIFEST}" "${z_fixture}"
}

rbte_suite() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  local z_suite="${BURD_TOKEN_3:-}"
  test -n "${z_suite}" || buc_die "No suite imprint — use tabtarget with imprint (e.g. rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh)"

  local z_fixture_list
  z_fixture_list="$(zrbte_resolve_suite "${z_suite}")"

  zrbte_build_binary

  local z_count=0
  local z_fixture
  for z_fixture in ${z_fixture_list}; do
    buc_step "Running theurge fixture '${z_fixture}'"
    "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" "${ZRBZ_COLOPHON_MANIFEST}" "${z_fixture}"
    z_count=$((z_count + 1))
  done

  buc_success "Suite '${z_suite}' complete (${z_count} fixtures)"
}

rbte_single() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  zrbte_build_binary

  local z_fixture="${1:-}"
  local z_case="${2:-}"
  "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" single "${ZRBZ_COLOPHON_MANIFEST}" ${z_fixture:+"${z_fixture}"} ${z_case:+"${z_case}"}
}

# eof

One design fork remains: the docket says **StateProgressing, sequentially-dependent cases (conjure→summon→run→abjure)**, which means multiple cases sharing the ephemeral hallmark across single-case-rerun boundaries. input.command: grep -n 'fn rbtdro_probe_reliquary_stamp\|reliquary-stamp.txt\|scratch\|fn rbtdro_scratch\|fn rbtdro_probe_root\|fn rbtdro_read_scratch\|RELIQUARY_SCRATCH\|fn rbtdro_persist' Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdro_onboarding.rs | head -30
input.description: Find scratch path resolution and probe

content.text:
I now have the complete shape. One genuine design fork surfaced that I should not silently resolve, because it contradicts an explicit docket instruction and materially shapes the code.

The docket prescribes **StateProgressing, four sequentially-dependent cases (conjure→summon→run→abjure)**. But the cited "strongest leverage" precedent — `rbtdrc_hallmark_lifecycle` — is a **single case** (Independent disposition) that threads the ephemeral hallmark as a local variable across its internal steps. The complication: the busybox hallmark is ephemeral (a content-addressed digest from the ordain BURV fact). The codebase's StateProgressing fixtures (onboarding) deliberately avoid out-of-source-tree scratch state and instead thread cross-case state through *committed regime files* — which don't exist for an ephemeral hallmark. So splitting into four standalone-rerunnable cases would require either a scratch-state mechanism the codebase otherwise shuns, or per-case re-discovery of the hallmark via audit.

Let me set up task tracking and get your call on this fork.

questions.question: The docket says StateProgressing/4 cases, but the cited precedent (hallmark_lifecycle) is single-case and the ephemeral hallmark has no durable cross-case home. Which fixture shape?

options.label: Single case (mirror hallmark_lifecycle)
options.description: One case threads the hallmark/refs as locals through ordain→summon→run→abjure. Independent disposition. Matches the cited precedent, simplest, 'mechanical'. Trades away standalone per-step rerun. (Recommended)
options.preview:
fn rbtdrd_build_run_lifecycle(dir) {
  probe(director cred present)
  (hm, gar, stem) = ordain(rbev-busybox)
  summon(hm); assert docker_inspect(ref)
  RUNTIME run --rm <ref> true => assert exit 0
  abjure(hm, BURE_CONFIRM=skip)
}
Disposition::Independent, 1 case

options.label: Four StateProgressing cases
options.description: ordain/summon/run/abjure as separate cases, each with a precondition probe, re-discovering the busybox hallmark via audit-hallmarks (no scratch file). Faithful to docket's literal wording; enables rerunning 'run' without re-ordaining, but adds per-case discovery machinery the codebase doesn't otherwise use.
options.preview:
case ordain  -> ordains, leaves hallmark in registry
case summon  -> probe: busybox hallmark in audit;
                re-discover hm, summon, assert local
case run     -> probe: image local; RUNTIME run true
case abjure  -> probe: hm present; abjure
Disposition::StateProgressing, 4 cases
(+ hallmark re-discovery in each case)

content.text:
Traced it. Here's what skirmish actually does and why it doesn't transfer.

**How skirmish commits hallmarks across cases**

In `onboarding-sequence` (the skirmish member that ordains), each ordain case captures the hallmark from the BURV fact and then **drives it into a consuming nameplate's `rbrn.env`** — `rbtdro_drive_hallmark(root, nameplate, RBRN_BOTTLE_HALLMARK|RBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK, hallmark)` at lines 908/961/1077/1220 — and commits that regime file. Downstream cases (the crucible charges) then read the hallmark back from the committed `rbrn.env`. That committed regime file *is* the durable cross-case home, which is why onboarding can be multi-case StateProgressing while honoring its own "no out-of-source-tree scratch state" rule.

**Does it make sense for dogfight? No — and for a structural reason, not a stylistic one.**

The pattern works in skirmish *because every ordained vessel has a consuming nameplate* (tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) whose `rbrn.env` is the natural committed home for its hallmark. I confirmed:

- The five nameplates are `ccyolo/moriah/pluml/srjcl/tadmor` — **none consumes busybox** (grep for `busybox` across all `rbrn.env` returns nothing).
- `rbev-busybox` is a consumerless vessel. To use the skirmish pattern, dogfight would have to invent a synthetic nameplate purely to park the hallmark line — which re-imports the nameplate/crucible apparatus that dogfight's locked constraint exists to *avoid*.

And the clincher: `hallmark_lifecycle` is the *other* fixture that ordains consumerless busybox, and it is **single-case** for exactly this reason — with no consuming nameplate, the hallmark has no durable home, so it threads as a local through the inline ordain→…→abjure sequence.

**So the investigation reinforces single-case.** It isn't a shortcut around the docket's StateProgressing wording; it's the same structural choice `hallmark_lifecycle` made under the identical "consumerless busybox" constraint. The docket's "sequentially dependent" is satisfied *within* the one case (the steps are ordered); StateProgressing-across-cases only earns its keep when there's committed state to progress, which dogfight deliberately lacks.

My recommendation: **single case, Independent disposition, mirroring `hallmark_lifecycle`** (swap its audit/rekon middle for summon + bare run). Want me to proceed on that basis?

15	// Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
16	//
17	// RBTDRB — precondition-probe helper for StateProgressing fixtures
18	//
19	// Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at entry.
20	// If the observed state matches the expected state the case body proceeds;
21	// otherwise the case returns a Fail verdict naming the expected state and
22	// the closest fixture that establishes it. The probe encapsulates state
23	// verification at the case level, so the engine does not need to track
24	// case history to support safe a-la-carte single-case rerun.
25	
26	use crate::rbtdre_engine::rbtdre_Verdict;
27	
28	/// A precondition probe attached to a StateProgressing case.
29	pub struct rbtdrb_Probe {
30	    /// Human-readable label for the precondition (e.g., "depot levied").
31	    pub name: &'static str,
32	    /// Check fn. Returns Ok(()) when the precondition holds; Err carries a
33	    /// diagnostic describing the observed state.
34	    pub check: fn() -> Result<(), String>,
35	    /// Operator-actionable next step naming the closest fixture or tabtarget
36	    /// that establishes the precondition.
37	    pub remediation: &'static str,
38	}
39	
40	/// Assert a probe at case entry.
41	///
42	/// On Ok(()) the precondition holds and the case body should proceed. On
43	/// Err(verdict) the case should return the verdict immediately — the verdict
44	/// names the unmet precondition and the remediation step.
45	pub fn rbtdrb_assert(probe: &rbtdrb_Probe) -> Result<(), rbtdre_Verdict> {
46	    match (probe.check)() {
47	        Ok(()) => Ok(()),
48	        Err(observed) => Err(rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!(
49	            "precondition '{}' not met: {}\nremediation: {}",
50	            probe.name, observed, probe.remediation
51	        ))),
52	    }
53	}
54

6	
7	# Bash Console Guide (BCG) - Pattern Reference
8	
9	## Core Philosophy
10	
11	This pattern emerged from environments where hidden failures cascade into production outages. When bash scripts manage critical infrastructure, every potential failure point needs explicit handling at its location, making post-mortem analysis straightforward.
12	
13	The aesthetic of elegant bash pipelines trades debuggability for brevity. A failing command in `$(echo | sed | awk)` leaves no trace. Temp files make each transformation step visible, testable, and recoverable.
14	
15	Traps and signal handlers create action-at-a-distance effects that obscure control flow. When error handling lives at the point of failure rather than in distant handlers, code review and maintenance become predictable.
16	
17	Enterprise bash isn't about looking clever; it's about being boringly, reliably correct. These patterns optimize for the 3am debugging session, not the initial writing experience. Each external process spawn is a potential failure point, so bash builtins are preferred despite verbosity.
18	
19	The patterns reject bash 4+ features to maintain compatibility with older enterprise systems still running bash 3.2, particularly older RHEL/CentOS or macOS deployments.
20	
21	### Load-Bearing Complexity
22	
23	Every element in a system — every function extraction, every pattern variant, every structural distinction — must carry weight. An element is **load-bearing** when its removal would create a gap between intent and behavior. When similar things differ, ask whether the difference is load-bearing: if yes, document why; if no, homogenize.
24	
25	This is the general decision criterion that tools like Zeroes Theory instantiate. Zeroes Theory asks "how many zeroes did this add to state space?" — load-bearing complexity asks the prior question: "does this element earn its existence at all?"
26	
27	Concrete examples from this codebase:
28	
29	- **Four separate `rbgi_add_*_iam` functions** look like copy-paste, but each wraps a different GCP API with different failure modes. The variation is load-bearing — extracting a "generic IAM helper" would hide which API failed at 3am.
30	- **Three copy-pasted Secret Manager IAM blocks** are NOT load-bearing variation — same API, same failure class, same recovery. Extract a helper.
31	- **Not adding three-part structure to a simple `api_enable` spec definition** — the structure would add ceremony without carrying meaning. Non-load-bearing elements increase cognitive cost without increasing correctness.
32	
33	Non-load-bearing elements are the silent budget overruns of a codebase. Each one is small; their aggregate is what makes systems incomprehensible.
34	
35	## Module Architecture
36	
37	Every module has an implementation file. CLI entry points are only present if module requires standalone execution.
38	
39	```bash
40	«prefix»_«name».sh (implementation - REQUIRED, prefer single lowercase word, allow snake_case)
41	«prefix»_cli.sh (entry point - OPTIONAL, omit for library/utility modules)
42	```
43	
44	### CLI as Module Gateway
45	
46	**Rule**: Code outside the BCG module system must never call `z*_kindle()` directly. All external access to BCG modules goes through CLI scripts.
47	
48	**Pattern**:
49	```
50	External Code → CLI script → furnish() kindles → module functions
51	```
52	
53	**Why this matters**:
54	- Kindle sequences are implementation details that may change
55	- The CLI's `furnish()` owns the kindle graph (which modules to kindle, in what order)
56	- External code remains stable when module dependencies change
57	- CLIs provide the documented, stable API for module functionality
58	
59	**Anti-pattern**:
60	```bash
61	# ❌ External script kindling directly
62	source "${TOOLS_DIR}/rbob_bottle.sh"
63	zrbob_kindle                          # VIOLATION: kindle leaked outside CLI
64	rbob_charge
65	```
66	
67	**Correct pattern**:
68	```bash
69	# ✅ External script delegates to CLI
70	exec "${TOOLS_DIR}/rbob_cli.sh" rbob_charge "$@"
71	```
72	
73	**What counts as "external code"**:
74	- Entry point scripts (workbenches, testbenches, coordinators)
75	- TabTargets and launchers
76	- Any dispatcher or router that invokes module functionality
77	- Scripts that are not themselves BCG modules
78	
79	**What can call kindle**:
80	- A module's own CLI (in `furnish()`)
81	- Another CLI's `furnish()` (when CLI A depends on module B, A's furnish kindles B)
82	- Test harnesses that explicitly isolate module behavior
83	
84	**Filesystem marker**: The `_cli.sh` suffix and the executable bit must agree. Within a BCG module directory:
85	
86	- Every `*_cli.sh` MUST be executable
87	- Every other `*.sh` MUST NOT be executable
88	
89	This makes the gateway role visible to `ls -l`: a workbench, testbench, or implementation module carrying `+x` is a bug — direct execution bypasses the CLI's `furnish()` and the kindle graph it owns. Tabtargets in `tt/` and shipped tool binaries are not BCG modules and stand outside this rule.
90	
91	### Module-CLI Prefix Unity
92	
93	**Convention**: A module and its CLI share a prefix (`rbrn_regime.sh` + `rbrn_cli.sh`).
94	
95	When commands within a single CLI have different dependency weights (e.g., `validate` needs
96	only the regime file, `audit` needs GCP/OAuth), use **differential furnish**: the furnish
97	function receives the command name as `$1` and conditionally sources/kindles heavy
98	dependencies based on which command is being invoked.
99	
100	### Regime Module Archetype
101	
102	A **regime** is a sourced `.env` file containing typed configuration fields — the contract between human-authored configuration and the modules that consume it. When a module's purpose is validating a regime, it follows the regime archetype:
103	
104	| Component | Location | Purpose |
105	|-----------|----------|---------|
106	| `buv_*_enroll` calls | kindle | Declare field contracts (type, range, description) |
107	| `buv_scope_sentinel` | kindle (last, before KINDLED) | Catch undeclared `PREFIX_*` variables |
108	| `z«prefix»_enforce` | regime module | `buv_vet SCOPE` + custom format checks |
109	| `readonly` lock | after enforce | Lock enrolled variables against mutation |
110	| `*_validate` command | CLI | `buv_report` — explained per-field display |
111	| `*_render` command | CLI | `buv_render` — diagnostic dump |
112	
113	Furnish sequence: kindle → enforce → lock (ironclad gate before any command runs). After enforce succeeds, lock all enrolled `PREFIX_*` variables with `readonly` to prevent accidental mutation by downstream code. Any derived state (e.g., docker env arrays) must be built from validated values after enforce, before or during the lock step.
114	
115	**Singleton** regimes source config unconditionally in furnish. **Manifold** regimes (multiple instances, e.g. nameplates) source conditionally when a folio identifies the instance.
116	
117	Enrollment types and gating API: see `buv_validation.sh`.
118	
119	---
120	
121	## Function Patterns
122	
123	### Boilerplate Functions (one per module/CLI)
124	
125	| Function             | Location       | First Line                                         | Can Source?       | Can use buc_step? | Purpose                                          |
126	|----------------------|----------------|----------------------------------------------------|-------------------|-------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
127	| `z«prefix»_kindle`   | Implementation | `test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | Yes (credentials) | No (use buc_log_*)| Define all kindle constants, set module state    |
128	| `z«prefix»_sentinel` | Implementation | `test "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" \|\| buc_die` | No                | No                | Guard all other functions                        |
129	| `z«prefix»_enforce`  | Implementation | `z«prefix»_sentinel`                               | No                | No                | Validate sourced config (regime archetype only)  |
130	| `z«prefix»_furnish`  | CLI only       | `buc_doc_env...`                                   | Yes (all deps)    | No                | Document env vars, source all deps, kindle       |
131	| Module header        | Implementation | `test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | No                | N/A               | Prevent multiple inclusion                       |
132	| CLI header           | CLI            | `set -euo pipefail`                                | Yes (buc_command)  | N/A               | Source buc_command.sh only                       |
133	
134	**Kindle constant**: Any variable — internal (`Z«PREFIX»_SCREAMING_NAME`) or public (`«PREFIX»_SCREAMING_NAME`) — defined exclusively within the kindle function with `readonly`. No other function — including enroll, setup, or helper functions — may assign to kindle constants. This ensures module state is fully determined at kindle time and visible in one place.
135	
136	**Mutable kindle state**: A variable initialized in kindle but intentionally mutated after kindle returns (counters, accumulators, registry rolls, builder state). Uses **lowercase** `z_«prefix»_name` to visually distinguish from `readonly` kindle constants. Never apply `readonly` to mutable kindle state.
137	
138	**Tinder constant**: A public variable (`«PREFIX»_lower_name`) defined at module top level, immediately after the `Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED=1` guard. Tinder constants must resolve to pure string literals at source time — **no runtime dependency, no `$(…)` computation, no expansion of kindle constants or runtime variables**. Tinder-on-tinder expansion is permitted: a tinder value may interpolate `${«PREFIX»_lower_name}` for any tinder constant defined earlier in the same module's tinder block, since that expansion resolves at source time and preserves the no-runtime-dependency invariant. They are available immediately after sourcing — no kindle required. Use `SCREAMING` case for the prefix (module identity) and `lower_snake` case for the name to visually distinguish from kindle constants. This enables sourcing chains where a tinder constant from module A provides the path for sourcing module B's config in the furnish function. The name reflects their role in the kindle metaphor: tinder is the fixed material prepared before kindling — inert, unchanging, and enabling the fire to start.
139	
140	**KINDLED must be last**: `readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1` must be the final statement in kindle. Since sentinel checks this variable, setting it last guarantees all kindle constants, roll arrays, and enroll calls are complete before the module is considered operational. Any function calling sentinel before kindle finishes will correctly fail. The `readonly` makes re-kindling a loud error.
141	
142	### Regular Functions (output via printouts/temp files)
143	
144	This table defines scope: «prefix»_* is public, z«prefix»_* is internal.
145	
146	| Type            | Pattern              | First Line           | Parameters | buc_step | Documentation        |
147	|-----------------|----------------------|----------------------|------------|----------|----------------------|
148	| Public          | `«prefix»_«command»` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | `"${1:-}"` | Yes      | Required (buc_doc_*) |
149	| Internal helper | `z«prefix»_«name»`   | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | `"${1:-}"` | Yes      | Recommended (buc_doc_*) |
150
